# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question]`
**Define "Notice Inviting Tender" (NIT) and list any four contents it must contain.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Notice Inviting Tender (NIT)** is a public advertisement issued by the client or tendering authority inviting interested and eligible contractors to submit bids for a specified landscape or construction work. It is the first formal document in the tendering process and ensures transparency and competitive bidding.

**Four essential contents of an NIT:**

1. **Name and description of the work** (e.g., "Development of Municipal Park at Ernakulam — Phase 1").
2. **Estimated cost** of the project and the **Earnest Money Deposit (EMD)** required.
3. **Period of completion** and the **last date and time** for submission of bids.
4. **Eligibility criteria** of bidders and the **date, time, and place** of bid opening.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question]`
**What is the Defect Liability Period (DLP) in a landscape contract? Why is it especially important for landscape works compared to building works?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Defect Liability Period (DLP)** is the specified duration after substantial completion of a project during which the contractor remains legally responsible for rectifying any defects, deficiencies, or failures in the executed work at no additional cost to the client.

**For landscape works, DLP is especially important because:**

1. **Plants are living materials** — they may die due to disease, inadequate aftercare, or poor planting technique. The DLP ensures the contractor replaces dead plants (subject to allowable mortality limit of 5–10%).
2. **Turf/grass establishment** takes time and requires proper maintenance; defects often emerge after a season.
3. **Irrigation systems** may leak, clog, or fail in initial months, requiring rectification.
4. **Hardscape elements** (pavers, kerbs) may settle unevenly due to soil movement.

Typically, **DLP for landscape works is 6 to 12 months** for softscape (plants, lawn) and up to **24 months** for hardscape elements.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Part B questions feature **internal choice**. Both alternatives are provided below with full sub-parts and model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question]`

**Explain in detail the complete Contract Procedure for a landscape project, with reference to:**
**(a) Preparation of tender documents and the role of the Bill of Quantities (7 Marks)**
**(b) Bid evaluation, contract award, and Defect Liability Period (7 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Tender Document Preparation & BOQ (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The contract procedure begins with the **preparation of tender documents**, which form the legal and technical foundation of the entire project. The key documents are:

1. **Notice Inviting Tender (NIT):** Public advertisement specifying work description, estimated cost, EMD, completion period, and bid submission deadline.

2. **Instructions to Bidders (ITB):** Rules for bid submission, eligibility, validity, and format.

3. **General Conditions of Contract (GCC):** Standard legal clauses governing all contracts (definitions, obligations, termination, dispute resolution).

4. **Special Conditions of Contract (SCC):** Project-specific clauses overriding or supplementing GCC.

5. **Technical Specifications:** Detailed material and workmanship standards for plants, soil, hardscape, and irrigation.

6. **Drawings:** Landscape layout, planting plan, irrigation network, sectional details.

7. **Bill of Quantities (BOQ):** A tabular document listing every item of work, its estimated quantity, unit, and rate.

**Role of the Bill of Quantities (BOQ):**
- Provides a **uniform basis** for all bidders to quote.
- Enables **fair comparison** of bids during evaluation.
- Forms the basis for **measurement and certification** of work done.
- Helps in **valuation of variations** during execution.
- For landscape works, typical BOQ items include: site clearance (sq.m), earthwork (cu.m), planting (per tree/shrub), turfing (sq.m), drip irrigation (running meter), pavers (sq.m), etc.

> **Examiner's Tip:** Students often forget to mention that **BOQ is prepared by the consultant/landscape architect**, not the contractor. This is a **1-mark penalty** issue.

#### **Part (b) — Bid Evaluation, Award, and DLP (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Bid Evaluation:**
- **Technical Evaluation:** Bids are first opened and checked for eligibility (experience, turnover, plant sourcing capacity, manpower). Non-eligible bids are rejected.
- **Financial Evaluation:** Financial bids of technically qualified bidders are opened. The **lowest (L1) bidder** is typically awarded the work in public sector tenders.
- **Reasonable Rate Check:** If L1 is "abnormally low" (say >10% below estimate), the tendering authority may negotiate or reject.

**Contract Award:**
- **Letter of Acceptance (LoA)** is issued to L1.
- The contractor submits **Performance Security (5–10% of contract value)**.
- A formal **Contract Agreement** is signed between client and contractor.
- **Mobilization Advance** (up to 10%) may be paid against bank guarantee.

**Execution, Measurement & Payments:**
- Work is executed as per drawings and specifications.
- **Engineer-in-Charge (E-in-C)** measures work and certifies **Running Account (RA) bills** (usually monthly).
- **Security Deposit (5–10%)** is retained from each RA bill.

**Defect Liability Period (DLP):**
- After substantial completion, the project enters DLP (typically **6–12 months** for softscape, up to **24 months** for hardscape).
- The contractor must rectify defects and replace dead plants exceeding allowable mortality (5–10%).
- After successful completion of DLP, **Performance Security is refunded** and the contract is formally closed.

> **Examiner's Tip:** Always mention that DLP is **distinct from the maintenance period**. Maintenance is an optional, separately paid service; DLP is mandatory and borne by the contractor.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question - Alternative Choice]`

**Discuss the following with respect to landscape contract management:**
**(a) Different types of contracts used in landscape projects with their suitability (7 Marks)**
**(b) Earnest Money Deposit, Performance Security, and Liquidated Damages — with a numerical example (7 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Types of Contracts (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Contract Type | Working Principle | Best Suited For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lump Sum (Fixed Price)** | Single fixed price for entire work | Well-defined packages (e.g., campus landscaping with known scope) | Risk of contractor cutting quality if underestimated |
| **Item Rate (Unit Price)** | Payment = Quantity executed × Unit rate | Government landscape tenders with variable scope | Final cost uncertain |
| **Cost Plus** | Actual cost + fixed fee/percentage | Restoration, ecological work with undefined scope | Less cost control for client |
| **Turnkey / Design-Build** | Single contractor for design + execution | Large private developments, integrated townships | Reduced design control for client |
| **Maintenance Contract** | Periodic payment for upkeep | Post-construction plant care, lawn mowing, irrigation servicing | Scope creep common |

**Suitability Discussion:**
- For **government landscape tenders** (Kerala PWD, Municipal Corporations), **Item Rate Contracts** are most common because the BOQ-based system allows flexibility and transparency.
- For **private residential landscape** projects, **Lump Sum** is preferred for cost certainty.
- For **ecological restoration or wetland projects**, **Cost Plus** is used because the work scope cannot be precisely defined upfront.

#### **Part (b) — EMD, PS, and LD with Numerical Example (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A landscape project has an **estimated cost of ₹60,00,000** and the **L1 contract value is ₹57,50,000**. The work is delayed by **30 days**. Compute EMD (2%), Performance Security (5%), and Liquidated Damages (0.5% per week of delay).

**Step 1 — Earnest Money Deposit (EMD):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMD} &= 60{,}00{,}000 \times 0.02 = 1{,}20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Performance Security (PS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PS} &= 57{,}50{,}000 \times 0.05 = 2{,}87{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Liquidated Damages (LD):**

Daily LD rate = 0.5% per week ÷ 7 = 0.0714% per day

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LD} &= 57{,}50{,}000 \times 0.000714 \times 30 \\
&\approx 1{,}23{,}171
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of Cap (10% max):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max LD} &= 57{,}50{,}000 \times 0.10 = 5{,}75{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

Since ₹ 1,23,171 < ₹ 5,75,000, the LD is **within limits**.

> **Examiner's Tip:** When solving LD problems, students often forget to convert weekly rate to daily rate. This costs **1–2 marks** consistently. Always show the conversion step explicitly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing EMD with Performance Security** — EMD is bid-stage; PS is post-award. Many students interchange them.
> 2. **Forgetting to state DLP duration** for softscape vs. hardscape — examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 3. **Not mentioning that LD has a 10% cap** — students often compute LD without checking the cap, losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the role of Engineer-in-Charge** in measurement and certification — this is a high-frequency question.
> 5. **Mixing up Lump Sum and Item Rate** contracts — remember: Lump Sum = one total price; Item Rate = quantity × rate.
> 6. **Ignoring pre-bid meeting** in tendering sequence — it is a mandatory step in most government tenders.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Contract Procedure** is a 4-phase lifecycle: Pre-Tender → Tendering → Award & Execution → Completion & Closure.
- **NIT (Notice Inviting Tender)** is the public advertisement that initiates the tendering process.
- **BOQ (Bill of Quantities)** is the most critical tender document — it standardizes bidding and forms the basis for measurement and payment.
- **EMD (Earnest Money Deposit)** = 1–2% of estimated cost, paid at bid stage.
- **Performance Security** = 5–10% of contract value, paid at award stage.
- **Mobilization Advance** = up to 10% of contract value, paid against bank guarantee.
- **Security Deposit (Retention)** = 5–10% deducted from each Running Account bill.
- **Liquidated Damages (LD)** = penalty for delay, usually 0.1–1% per day, capped at 10% of contract value.
- **Defect Liability Period (DLP)** = 6–12 months for softscape, up to 24 months for hardscape.
- **Plant Mortality Limit** in DLP = typically 5–10% of total planted quantity.
- **5 Contract Types** to remember: Lump Sum, Item Rate, Cost Plus, Turnkey, Maintenance.
- **Item Rate Contract** is the **most common** in government landscape tenders in Kerala.
- **Engineer-in-Charge (E-in-C)** is the key certifying authority for measurements and RA bills.
- **Letter of Acceptance (LoA)** is issued to L1 bidder; contract agreement is signed only after Performance Security is submitted.
- **Variations** beyond ±25% of BOQ quantity typically require fresh approval/rate analysis.
- **Dispute Resolution** hierarchy: Amicable settlement → Conciliation → Arbitration → Litigation.
- For KTU exam: Always draw a **flowchart of contract procedure** if the question is worth 7+ marks — diagrams earn easy marks.
- Remember the **distinction between DLP and Maintenance Period** — DLP is mandatory contractor obligation; Maintenance is optional paid service.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Criteria for Selecting Contractors: The Process of Calling Tenders, Comparison of Various Kinds of Tenders with Regard to Objectives, Utility and Appropriateness, Tender Documentation and Evaluation of Tender, Negotiations with Contractors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT612 - LANDSCAPE DESIGN AND MANGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Criteria for Selecting Contractors: The Process of Calling Tenders, Comparison of Various Kinds of Tenders with Regard to Objectives, Utility and Appropriateness, Tender Documentation and Evaluation of Tender, Negotiations with Contractors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Contractor Selection and Tendering** is the formal, regulated procurement procedure by which a client (owner/principal) invites qualified construction firms (contractors) to submit competitive bids for executing a defined scope of landscape, civil, or architectural works, and subsequently evaluates these bids against predetermined technical, financial, legal, and managerial criteria to award the contract to the most suitable bidder.

In the context of **OEAGT612 – Landscape Design and Management**, this topic governs how landscape projects (gardens, parks, urban green spaces, irrigation networks, hardscape elements, plantation works, and site development) are procured, contracted, and executed through external agencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> "Tendering is a structured invitation-to-bid process wherein an employer obtains competitive offers from prequalified contractors, evaluates them transparently against published criteria, and awards work to the bidder whose offer best satisfies the stated objectives of cost, quality, time, and compliance."

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are organizing a large **wedding feast for 500 guests in Kerala**. You cannot cook everything yourself. So you ask three caterers:
1. To give you a written quotation (their **bid/tender**).
2. To specify the menu, timing, cost, and past wedding photos (their **technical + financial proposal**).
3. You compare the quotations not only on price but also on hygiene rating, past client reviews, and quality of ingredients.

The lowest price is **not always** the best choice — the cheapest caterer may use stale ingredients or arrive late. The process of *inviting, comparing, and negotiating* with caterers before signing the deal is exactly what **tendering and contractor selection** means in landscape construction.

In landscape projects, the "caterer" is the **landscape contractor**, the "wedding venue" is the **project site**, and the "menu card" is the **Bill of Quantities (BoQ)** with specifications for plants, soil, paving, lighting, and irrigation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** A tender is NOT just a price tag — it is a comprehensive proposal bundling **price, time, method, quality plan, and legal commitment**. Selecting a contractor is a *risk-mitigation decision*, not a *price-minimization decision*.

---

### 1.3 Key Stakeholders & Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Employer / Owner / Client** | Entity that commissions the landscape project (e.g., government body, developer, private estate owner) |
| **Tenderer / Bidder** | Contractor who submits the tender |
| **Consultant / Landscape Architect** | Designs the project, prepares drawings, BoQ, and supervises execution |
| **Engineer-in-Charge (EIC)** | Representative of the client who certifies measurements, payments, and quality |
| **Bid Security / Earnest Money Deposit (EMD)** | Refundable deposit submitted with the tender to discourage frivolous bids |
| **Performance Security** | Bank guarantee taken from the selected contractor to ensure project completion |
| **Letter of Acceptance (LoA)** | Formal letter informing the successful bidder of award |
| **Letter of Intent (LoI)** | Preliminary letter before formal LoA is issued |
| **Agreement / Contract Document** | Legally binding document signed between client and contractor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Standard Metric:** As per CPWD and KTU-aligned public procurement norms, **Earnest Money is typically 2% of the estimated tender value**, and **Performance Security is 5%–10% of the contract value**, valid until defect liability period ends.

---

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters for Landscape Projects

Landscape works involve **biological components (plants, turf, trees)** that are highly time-sensitive and perishable. A wrong contractor can:
- Substitute plant species silently (cost reduction malpractice).
- Use inferior soil/media.
- Delay plantation beyond the optimal monsoon window.
- Execute poor drainage leading to plant mortality.

Therefore, **contractor selection is a critical success factor** — not an administrative formality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Criteria for Selecting Contractors (The "6-C Framework")

Contractors are evaluated against **six universal criteria** (the **6-C Framework**), equally applicable to landscape, building, and infrastructure works.

1. **Competence** — Technical capability, manpower, equipment.
2. **Capacity** — Financial strength, ability to mobilize resources.
3. **Capital** — Working capital, creditworthiness, solvency.
4. **Credibility** — Past performance, track record, reputation.
5. **Compliance** — Legal, statutory, safety, and environmental compliance.
6. **Cost-Competitiveness** — Competitive yet realistic pricing (suspiciously low bids signal risk).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A 14-mark question often asks students to *list and explain the criteria for contractor selection*. Memorize this **6-C framework** as a structured answer.

---

### 2.2 Pre-Qualification (Prequalification) — The Gatekeeping Stage

Before tenders are issued, the employer shortlists eligible contractors through **Prequalification (PQ)**.

**Purpose:** Eliminate contractors who lack the financial, technical, or experiential capacity to deliver the project.

**Typical PQ Documents Submitted by Contractor:**
- Company profile, registration certificates.
- Audited financial statements (last 3–5 years).
- List of similar works executed in last 5–7 years (with values).
- List of equipment and machinery owned.
- Manpower details (engineers, horticulturists, supervisors).
- Bank solvency certificate.
- Affidavit of no litigation/blacklisting.
- GST, PAN, EPF, ESI registrations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prequalification Pass-Fail Principle:** PQ is a *pass/fail* exercise — it does NOT rank contractors; it merely filters eligible ones for the next bidding stage.

---

### 2.3 The Process of Calling Tenders — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Project Preparation**
- Completion of landscape design, drawings, and detailed estimate.
- Approval of funding, statutory clearances, and land handover.

**Step 2: Preparation of Tender Documents**
- Drafting of Notice Inviting Tender (NIT).
- Preparation of Bill of Quantities (BoQ), specifications, drawings, conditions of contract.

**Step 3: Mode of Tender (Decide Open/Limited/Single/Negotiated)**
- Decision based on project value, urgency, complexity, and confidentiality.

**Step 4: Publication / Invitation**
- Published in newspapers, official websites, e-procurement portals (e.g., Government e-Marketplace GeM, Kerala e-Procurement).

**Step 5: Sale of Tender Documents**
- Sold to eligible parties for a non-refundable fee against a receipt.

**Step 6: Pre-Bid Meeting**
- Clarifications issued by the client/consultant; minutes circulated to all bidders for transparency.

**Step 7: Receipt of Bids**
- Sealed quotations received up to a fixed date and time.

**Step 8: Bid Opening**
- Public opening in presence of attending bidders (for open tenders).

**Step 9: Evaluation of Tenders**
- Technical, financial, and combined evaluation per published criteria.

**Step 10: Award of Work**
- Issue of Letter of Acceptance (LoA), submission of performance security, signing of agreement, handing over site.

---

### 2.4 Comparison of Various Kinds of Tenders

This is the **highest-weightage sub-topic** in the module — students must know each tender type's *objective, utility, and appropriateness*.

| Type of Tender | Objective | Utility | Appropriateness / When Used | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Open Tender** | Maximum competition, transparency | Public sector works, large landscape projects | All eligible contractors can bid; ensures fair competition | Higher administrative cost, may attract unqualified bidders |
| **Limited Tender (Restricted)** | Quality control over participants | Specialized landscape works (theme parks, golf courses) | Invitation sent to a shortlist of prequalified contractors only | Reduced competition may inflate cost |
| **Negotiated Tender (Single-Source / Nomination)** | Speed and confidentiality | Emergency repairs, defense, critical heritage landscapes | Employer negotiates directly with one chosen contractor | Risk of favoritism, lacks transparency |
| **Sealed Bid Tender** | Confidentiality of bid price | Standard government procurement | Bids opened publicly on a fixed date | No negotiation allowed; rigidity |
| **Electronic Tender (e-Tender)** | Efficiency, paperless audit trail | Modern mandatory practice (GeM, e-procurement) | Online submission, evaluation, and award | Requires digital literacy, connectivity |
| **Two-Stage Tender** | First-stage design refinement, then price bidding | Complex landscape design-build (D&B) projects | Stage 1: Technical proposal; Stage 2: Price bid from shortlist | Longer timeline, higher transaction cost |
| **Turnkey / EPC Tender** | Single-point accountability | Large integrated landscape + infrastructure | Contractor designs, builds, and hands over operational asset | Contractor bears more risk, hence higher price |
| **Framework Agreement (Rate Contract)** | Long-term readiness for repetitive works | Annual maintenance contracts (AMC) for parks | Pre-agreed rates for repeated call-offs | Idle if work volume is low |
| **Package / Lump-Sum Tender** | Fixed-price simplicity | Small landscape works, residential gardens | Single price for entire defined scope | Risk of contractor cutting quality |
| **Item-Rate / Unit-Price Tender** | Flexibility when quantities are uncertain | Landscape works with variable plantation quantities | BoQ-based pricing; payment as per actual quantity executed | Final cost can overrun estimate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiners frequently test:** *"Compare Open Tender and Limited Tender"* or *"Discuss the appropriateness of Negotiated Tender in landscape works."* Memorize the **Objective, Utility, Appropriateness** triplet for each type.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Procurement Cost Calculations)

Although this is largely qualitative, certain quantitative procurement parameters frequently appear in numerical problems.

$$
\text{EMD (Earnest Money Deposit)} = 2\% \times \text{Estimated Tender Value (ETV)}
$$

$$
\text{Performance Security} = 5\% \text{ to } 10\% \times \text{Contract Value (CV)}
$$

$$
\text{Defect Liability Period (DLP)} = 12 \text{ to } 24 \text{ months for landscape works}
$$

$$
\text{Bid Validity Period} = 90 \text{ to } 180 \text{ days (standard)}
$$

$$
\text{Tendered Rate \% below/above} = \frac{\text{Estimated Cost} - \text{Bid Value}}{\text{Estimated Cost}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Lowest Quoted Rate (\%)} = \frac{L_1 - \text{Estimate}}{E} \times 100
$$

where $L_1$ = lowest evaluated bid value, $E$ = engineer’s estimate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula Use in KTU Exam:** If a question states *"The estimated cost of a landscape project is ₹50 lakhs, and the lowest bid is ₹43 lakhs,"* then the percentage quote below estimate is:
> $$\frac{50 - 43}{50} \times 100 = 14\%$$
> If this falls below the prescribed threshold (typically 10–15% below estimate), the bid is considered *unreasonably low* and may be rejected or the contractor asked to deposit additional performance security.

---

### 2.6 Tender Documentation — Anatomy of a Tender Document

A complete **tender document package** consists of:

| Section | Contents |
|---|---|
| **Section 1: Notice Inviting Tender (NIT)** | Project name, location, value, EMD, submission deadline, eligibility |
| **Section 2: Instructions to Bidders (ITB)** | Bid preparation, submission rules, validity, opening procedure |
| **Section 3: Forms of Bid** | Letter of tender, qualification forms, integrity pact |
| **Section 4: Conditions of Contract** | General Conditions (GCC) + Special Conditions (SCC) |
| **Section 5: Technical Specifications** | Material specs (plants, soil, paving, lighting, irrigation) |
| **Section 6: Bill of Quantities (BoQ)** | Item-wise quantities and rates to be quoted |
| **Section 7: Drawings** | Site plan, planting plan, hard landscape, irrigation layout |
| **Section 8: Appendices** | Schedules, formats of securities, agreement format |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Landscape-Specific Tender Addenda:** A landscape tender must include:
> - Species list with botanical names.
> - Plant size at delivery (girth, height, root-ball specification).
> - Soil/media specifications (pH, organic content, drainage).
> - Irrigation system make and model.
> - Maintenance period and survival guarantee clause (typically 6–12 months post-planting).

---

### 2.7 Evaluation of Tender — The "3-Stage Evaluation Protocol"

**Stage 1: Preliminary Examination**
- Receipt of bid on time, in correct format, with EMD.
- Bids failing preliminary checks are *rejected outright* (responsive vs. non-responsive).

**Stage 2: Technical Evaluation**
- Compliance with specifications, methodology, plant sourcing plan, irrigation design, manpower deployment schedule.
- Bid may be *technically qualified* or *technically disqualified*.

**Stage 3: Financial Evaluation**
- Among technically qualified bidders, the lowest evaluated cost (L1) wins, subject to reasonableness check.

**Reasonable Rate Concept:** A bid that is abnormally low (e.g., > 10% below estimate) triggers *additional scrutiny*. The contractor may be required to demonstrate capability to execute at the quoted rate, or extra performance security may be demanded.

> [!NOTE]
> **Combined Quality-Cum-Cost Selection (QCBS):** Some tenders use a **weighted score**:
> $$\text{Total Score} = (W_t \times T) + (W_f \times F)$$
> where $W_t$ = weight of technical score (e.g., 70%), $W_f$ = weight of financial score (e.g., 30%), $T$ = normalized technical score, $F$ = normalized financial score.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Tender Type Selection Logic

The choice of tender type depends on four situational variables:

1. **Project Value** (High / Medium / Low)
2. **Project Urgency** (Emergency / Normal / Long-term)
3. **Project Complexity** (Specialized / Standard)
4. **Confidentiality Requirement** (Public / Sensitive)

| Project Profile | Recommended Tender Type | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Large public landscape (e.g., Smart City park, ₹10+ cr) | **Open / e-Tender (Two-Stage)** | Maximum transparency, statutory mandate |
| Private villa garden, ₹20 lakh | **Limited Tender** | Quality control, manageable bidder pool |
| Emergency landslide restoration in landscape | **Negotiated Tender** | Speed, no time for bidding cycle |
| Botanical garden with rare species | **Limited / Two-Stage** | Specialized horticulture expertise needed |
| Annual maintenance of campus gardens | **Framework / Rate Contract** | Repeated call-offs at agreed rates |
| Design-build landscape mega-project | **Turnkey / EPC Tender** | Single-point accountability |
| Heritage site sensitive works | **Negotiated with Nominated Specialist** | Confidentiality + skill preservation |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Ready Phrasing:** "The appropriateness of a tender type is determined by the interplay of project size, complexity, urgency, and confidentiality requirements — there is no universal 'best' tender type."

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical: Tendered Rate Percentage Calculation

**Problem:**
The estimated cost of a landscape development project in a Kerala municipality is **₹1,20,00,000**. Three contractors submit bids:

- Contractor A: ₹1,14,00,000
- Contractor B: ₹1,05,00,000
- Contractor C: ₹1,08,00,000

Determine:
(i) The percentage quoted by each contractor below the estimate.
(ii) The lowest bidder (L1).
(iii) Whether the L1 bid is "reasonably low" assuming the threshold for abnormal low bid is 12% below estimate.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Percentage Quote Formula:**
$$
\text{Quote (\% below estimate)} = \frac{E - B}{E} \times 100
$$
where $E$ = estimate, $B$ = bid value.

**Step 2 — Contractor A:**
$$
\text{Quote}_A = \frac{1{,}20{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}14{,}00{,}000}{1{,}20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 5.00\%
$$

**Step 3 — Contractor B:**
$$
\text{Quote}_B = \frac{1{,}20{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}05{,}00{,}000}{1{,}20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 12.50\%
$$

**Step 4 — Contractor C:**
$$
\text{Quote}_C = \frac{1{,}20{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}08{,}00{,}000}{1{,}20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 10.00\%
$$

**Step 5 — Identify L1:**
The lowest bid is Contractor B at ₹1,05,00,000. Hence, **Contractor B = L1**.

**Step 6 — Reasonableness Check (Threshold = 12%):**
$$
\text{Quote}_B = 12.50\% > 12.00\% \text{ (threshold)}
$$
Therefore, **Contractor B's bid is abnormally low** and triggers additional scrutiny. The client may:
- Reject the bid (justified under abnormal-low-bid clause).
- OR ask the contractor to provide an *Additional Performance Security* equal to the difference between the estimated and quoted amount:
$$
\text{Additional Performance Security} = E - B = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}05{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}15{,}00{,}000
$$

**Final Recommendation:**
If the additional security is provided, the contract may be awarded to Contractor B; otherwise, the contract may be awarded to the next lowest evaluated bidder (Contractor C at ₹1,08,00,000).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often wrongly calculate the percentage as (Bid / Estimate) × 100. The correct reference is **percentage BELOW estimate**, not percentage OF estimate. Memorize the formula:
> $$\text{Quote \%} = \frac{E - B}{E} \times 100$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical: Earnest Money and Performance Security

**Problem:**
A landscape project has estimated cost ₹80,00,000. Calculate EMD and Performance Security as per standard norms (EMD = 2%, Performance Security = 5%).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — EMD:**
$$
\text{EMD} = \frac{2}{100} \times 80{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}60{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Performance Security:**
$$
\text{Performance Security} = \frac{5}{100} \times 80{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}4{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Total Refundable Deposits Paid by Successful Bidder:**
$$
\text{Total Deposit} = 1{,}60{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}5{,}60{,}000
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** EMD is refunded (without interest) to unsuccessful bidders after bid acceptance. For the successful bidder, EMD is *adjusted* against the Performance Security, so the net additional outflow is only ₹2,40,000.

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Tender Evaluation Flowchart (Textual Derivation)

1. **Open Tenders** → Receive all bids.
2. **Preliminary Check** → Late bids, unsigned bids, missing EMD → REJECT.
3. **Responsive Check** → Does the bid comply with all instructions? If NO → REJECT.
4. **Technical Evaluation** → Does it meet specifications? If NO → DISQUALIFY (technically).
5. **Financial Evaluation (only for technically qualified)**:
   - Arithmetical check for errors (if 10 × 100 written instead of 1000, rectify or reject).
   - Apply price preference policies (if applicable to MSEs, local firms).
   - Apply abnormally low bid clause.
6. **Identify L1 (Lowest Evaluated Bidder)**.
7. **Verify Reasonableness, Pre-Qualification, Past Performance**.
8. **Issue Letter of Acceptance (LoA)**.
9. **Sign Agreement after Performance Security Deposit**.

---

### 3.5 Negotiation with Contractors — Permissibility and Limits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** In public/government tenders, **post-tender negotiation is generally PROHIBITED** to ensure transparency and prevent corruption. Award MUST go to the lowest evaluated responsive bidder (L1). Negotiations are permitted ONLY in:
> - Negotiated/single-source tenders.
> - Proprietary/nominated works.
> - Where ALL bids exceed the estimate and re-tendering is not feasible.

**Permitted Negotiation Strategies (where allowed):**
1. **Rate Negotiation:** Bringing the L1 rate closer to the estimate.
2. **Scope Reduction:** Reducing quantity/scope to match available budget.
3. **Split Award:** Dividing work among multiple bidders.
4. **Combined Award with Package Discount:** Bundling works to leverage better rates.
5. **Performance-linked Negotiations:** Tying final payment to survival of plantation, warranty periods, etc.

**Documentation:** Every negotiation MUST be recorded in **Minutes of Meeting (MoM)** signed by both parties — a KTU exam favourite for procedure-based questions.

### 4.1 Tendering Process — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "Tender" and explain its importance in landscape construction projects.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A *Tender* is a formal, written offer submitted by a contractor in response to an invitation by the employer to execute a defined scope of landscape construction or maintenance work at a quoted price within a stipulated time.

**Importance in landscape projects:**
1. Ensures **competitive pricing** through transparent comparison of bids.
2. Establishes a **legally binding contract** that protects both parties.
3. Provides a mechanism to verify the contractor's **technical and financial capability** before award.
4. Facilitates **quality assurance** through published specifications and BoQ.

*(Scoring: Definition 1.5 marks + any 3 points of importance = 1.5 marks)*

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**What is Prequalification of Contractors? Why is it conducted?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Prequalification (PQ)* is the preliminary evaluation of contractors against financial, technical, and legal criteria to determine their eligibility to receive tender documents.

**Why conducted:**
1. To **eliminate incapable or inexperienced contractors** before detailed bidding.
2. To ensure only contractors with adequate **resources, manpower, and equipment** compete.
3. To reduce the risk of **project failure, delay, or abandonment** due to contractor insolvency.
4. To enhance **quality and reliability** of bids received.

*(Scoring: Definition 1 mark + 3 reasons = 2 marks)*

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **various criteria for selecting contractors** for landscape construction works. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Open Tender, Limited Tender, and Negotiated Tender** with respect to their objectives, utility, and appropriateness. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Criteria for Selecting Contractors (7 marks):**

The selection of a landscape contractor is a multi-criteria decision. The widely accepted **6-C framework** is:

1. **Competence (Technical Capability):**
   Possession of qualified horticulturists, landscape engineers, irrigation specialists; experience in similar projects (botanical gardens, urban parks, golf courses); knowledge of local agro-climatic conditions.

2. **Capacity (Resources):**
   Adequate manpower, owned/leased equipment (JCBs, tractors, sprinklers, pruning tools), plant nurseries or tie-ups for sourcing genuine species.

3. **Capital (Financial Strength):**
   Solvency certificate from bank, working capital to mobilize site within 30 days, no outstanding debts. Financial statements audited for last 3–5 years.

4. **Credibility (Track Record):**
   Past performance, client testimonials, completion certificates, no history of blacklisting, litigation, or abandonment.

5. **Compliance (Statutory & Legal):**
   Valid registration, GST, PAN, EPF, ESI, labour license, environmental clearances for sensitive ecosystems.

6. **Cost Competitiveness:**
   Reasonable bid price — neither abnormally high (inflated) nor abnormally low (risk of abandonment). The contractor must demonstrate capability to execute at the quoted rate.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** Naming the 6 Cs with one-line description each is sufficient for 6 marks. Add a concluding sentence about composite evaluation for the 7th mark.

---

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Open Tender | Limited Tender | Negotiated Tender |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Maximum competition and transparency | Quality control over participants | Speed and confidentiality |
| **Utility** | Large public-sector landscape projects | Specialized horticultural works, restricted bidder pool | Emergency repairs, critical/heritage works |
| **Appropriateness** | Suitable when statutory transparency is mandatory | Suitable when only a few specialists can execute | Suitable when bidding cycle cannot be completed |
| **Bidder Pool** | Open to all eligible contractors | Invitation-only from prequalified list | Single nominated contractor |
| **Transparency** | Highest | Moderate | Lowest |
| **Cost Outcome** | Usually lowest competitive price | Moderate pricing | Higher risk of inflated price |
| **Risk of Favoritism** | Negligible | Low | High |
| **Time Taken** | Longest (advertising + bidding cycle) | Moderate | Shortest |

**Conclusion:** The choice depends on the project's value, urgency, complexity, and confidentiality. No single tender type is universally superior; each has its contextual fit.

*(Scoring: Table covers 5 marks + objective/utility/appropriateness emphasis 1 mark + conclusion 1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]`**

**(a)** Describe the **complete process of calling tenders** for a landscape development project. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **three-stage evaluation of tenders** and the concept of **negotiation with contractors**. When are negotiations legally and ethically permissible? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Process of Calling Tenders (7 marks):**

1. **Project Preparation (1 mark):** Completion of landscape design, drawings, detailed estimate, statutory approvals, and funding sanction.
2. **Preparation of Tender Documents (1 mark):** Drafting NIT, ITB, BoQ, specifications, drawings, conditions of contract, agreement draft.
3. **Decision on Tender Type (0.5 mark):** Open / Limited / Negotiated / e-Tender, based on size and urgency.
4. **Publication / Invitation (0.5 mark):** Advertisement in newspapers, website, e-procurement portal; minimum notice period of 14–21 days.
5. **Sale of Tender Documents (0.5 mark):** Sold at a non-refundable fee against receipt.
6. **Pre-Bid Meeting (0.5 mark):** Held to clarify bidder queries; minutes circulated to all bidders.
7. **Receipt and Opening of Bids (1 mark):** Sealed bids received up to deadline; opened publicly; bidders may attend.
8. **Evaluation and Award (1 mark):** Three-stage evaluation → Letter of Acceptance → Performance Security → Agreement → Site Handover.

---

**(b) Three-Stage Tender Evaluation & Negotiation (7 marks):**

**Stage 1 – Preliminary Examination (1 mark):**
- Check bid receipt time, format, signatures, EMD submission, validity period.
- Late, conditional, or unsigned bids rejected.

**Stage 2 – Technical Evaluation (2 marks):**
- Verify compliance with technical specifications (plant species, sizes, soil media, irrigation components).
- Check methodology, planting schedule, maintenance plan, manpower.
- Disqualify non-responsive technical bids.

**Stage 3 – Financial Evaluation (2 marks):**
- Among technically qualified bidders, identify lowest (L1).
- Apply arithmetical corrections, price preference policies.
- Apply **abnormally low bid clause** — if L1 > 10–15% below estimate, demand additional performance security or reject.

**Negotiation with Contractors (2 marks):**

Negotiation refers to post-bid discussions to refine rates, scope, or terms before contract signing.

**Permissible scenarios:**
- Negotiated/single-source tenders (always permitted).
- All bids exceed estimate and re-tendering is impractical.
- Proprietary/nominated items.
- Emergency works.

**Impermissible in open public tenders** (to ensure transparency and prevent corruption).

**Mandatory documentation:** Minutes of Meeting (MoM) signed by both parties.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often confuse **negotiation** with **corruption**. Always clarify that negotiation is a *legal procedural tool* used **only** in defined scenarios.
> - Many students skip the **abnormally low bid clause**, which carries high weightage. Always mention it when discussing financial evaluation.
> - Avoid using one-word answers for 14-mark questions — structure with headings, bullets, and tables.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **6-C Framework** (Competence, Capacity, Capital, Credibility, Compliance, Cost) is the universal contractor selection criterion.
- ✅ **Prequalification** is a *pass/fail* filter — it does NOT rank contractors.
- ✅ **Tender types to memorize:** Open, Limited, Negotiated, Two-Stage, e-Tender, Turnkey/EPC, Framework/Rate Contract, Item-Rate, Lump-Sum.
- ✅ Each tender type must be explained with its **Objective, Utility, and Appropriateness** triad.
- ✅ **EMD = 2% of estimate**; **Performance Security = 5–10% of contract value** (bold numbers for exam).
- ✅ **Tender evaluation = 3 stages:** Preliminary → Technical → Financial (L1 selection with reasonableness check).
- ✅ **Negotiation is generally PROHIBITED in public tenders** — permitted only in specific scenarios listed above.
- ✅ **Landscape-specific tender addenda:** Species list, plant sizes, soil specs, irrigation specs, survival guarantee clause.
- ✅ **Always document** every negotiation through signed MoM.
- ✅ **Standard formula:** $\text{Quote \% below estimate} = \frac{E - B}{E} \times 100$
- ✅ **Bid Validity Period:** 90–180 days standard.
- ✅ **Defect Liability Period (DLP)** for landscape works: typically 12–24 months (plant survival guarantee clause falls here).
- ✅ **Bid opening** must be public; non-attending bidders cannot later challenge the process.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Tender Types — "OLN TEF LI":** Open, Limited, Negotiated, Two-stage, EPC/Turnkey, Framework, Lump-sum, Item-rate.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Contract Documentation: Forms of Contract, General and Special Conditions, Specifications, Bill of Quantities, Significant Clauses Pertaining to Defects, Maintenance, Arbitrations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT612 - LANDSCAPE DESIGN AND MANGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Contract Documentation: Forms of Contract, General and Special Conditions, Specifications, Bill of Quantities, Significant Clauses Pertaining to Defects, Maintenance, Arbitrations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Contract Documentation

**Contract Documentation** in landscape engineering refers to the complete, legally binding set of written instruments, drawings, and technical records that define the rights, obligations, risks, deliverables, payment terms, and quality benchmarks between the **Client (Employer)** and the **Contractor** for the execution of a landscape project — covering everything from site grading and plantation works to hardscape installation and long-term maintenance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Contract documentation is the *single source of legal and technical truth* on a landscape project. It governs how a garden, park, urban plaza, or ecological restoration is actually built, paid for, inspected, maintained, and — if things go wrong — disputed and resolved.

The core components covered in this module are:

- **Forms of Contract** (the legal skeleton)
- **General Conditions of Contract (GCC)** (the universal rules)
- **Special Conditions of Contract (SCC)** (the project-specific rules)
- **Specifications** (the quality DNA)
- **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)** (the commercial spine)
- **Significant Clauses** — Defects, Maintenance, Arbitration (the safety nets)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Wedding Planner Contract"

Imagine you are hiring a wedding planner to organize an outdoor garden wedding:

- The **Form of Contract** is the *type of agreement* you sign — a fixed-price package, a per-guest rate, or an open-ended time-and-materials deal.
- The **General Conditions** are the *standard rules of any wedding* — "no-shows forfeit deposit, vendor substitutions require notice."
- The **Special Conditions** are *your specific rules* — "the roses must be red, the stage must face east, the music must stop by 10 PM."
- The **Specifications** describe *quality* — "100 red roses, premium grade, 24-inch stems."
- The **Bill of Quantities** is the *shopping list with prices* — 100 roses × ₹50 = ₹5,000; 1 stage × ₹25,000 = ₹25,000; Total = ₹3,20,000.
- The **Defects Clause** says, "if the roses wilt within 24 hours, the planner replaces them free."
- The **Maintenance Clause** says, "the planner returns on Day 2 to clean up."
- The **Arbitration Clause** says, "if we fight, we go to a neutral wedding arbitrator, not court."

Replace *wedding* with *landscape project* and you have the entire universe of contract documentation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard:** Most Indian landscape and civil works follow the **CPWD Form of Contract**, **MORTH Specifications**, or **FIDIC Conditions of Contract (Red/Yellow/Silver Book)** as the backbone, with landscape-specific clauses added in the SCC.

### 1.3 Why Contract Documentation is Non-Negotiable in Landscape Projects

Landscape works are uniquely risky because they involve:

- **Living materials** (plants, turf, trees) that can die — raising disputes over *defects*.
- **Long establishment periods** (3 years for trees) — raising disputes over *maintenance*.
- **Subjective quality judgments** (aesthetics, growth, vigour) — raising disputes over *specifications*.
- **Multiple sub-trades** (irrigation, civil, horticulture, lighting) — raising disputes over *coordination*.

Hence, robust documentation is the only insurance against litigation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Forms of Contract (The Legal Skeleton)

A **Form of Contract** is the standard template that defines *how the contractor is paid* and *how risk is allocated*. The four classical forms are:

#### 2.1.1 Lump Sum (Fixed Price) Contract

- Contractor quotes a **single fixed price** for the entire scope.
- Best for: clearly defined, low-risk landscape works (e.g., a defined park with a finished design).
- Risk: high on contractor if quantities are misjudged.
- Example: ₹1.2 Crore for a 2-acre sensory garden, complete.

#### 2.1.2 Cost Plus Contract

- Contractor is reimbursed **actual cost + an agreed fee/percentage**.
- Best for: undefined, evolving scopes (e.g., ecological restoration where surprises are common).
- Risk: high on employer (low incentive for contractor efficiency).
- Example: Actual cost + 12% overhead + 8% profit.

#### 2.1.3 Unit Rate (Item Rate) Contract

- Contractor quotes **rates per unit** (per sq.m, per tree, per cum of excavation).
- Quantities are measured from the BOQ.
- Best for: landscape works where quantities can be reliably measured.
- Example: ₹450 per sq.m of lawn; ₹1,200 per tree including 1-year maintenance.

#### 2.1.4 Turnkey / Design-Build Contract

- Single contractor handles **both design and construction**.
- Best for: integrated landscape + irrigation + lighting packages.
- Risk: requires a clearly written performance specification.

### 2.2 General Conditions of Contract (GCC)

The GCC is a **boilerplate legal document** containing clauses that apply to *every* contract of that standard form. Typical GCC includes:

- Definitions and interpretations
- Obligations of the employer and contractor
- Scope of work and variations
- Measurement and valuation
- Payment terms and certificates
- Delays, extensions of time, and liquidated damages
- Suspension and termination
- Defects Liability Period
- Force majeure
- Settlement of disputes and arbitration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **GCC does not change between projects** of the same organization. Only the **Special Conditions** vary.

### 2.3 Special Conditions of Contract (SCC)

The SCC **amends, supplements, or overrides** the GCC for a specific project. In landscape projects, SCCs typically specify:

- Project location, climatic zone, and soil conditions
- Plant species list, sizes, and sourcing nurseries
- Water-availability windows for irrigation
- Restricted working hours (e.g., near hospitals or heritage zones)
- Local authority (municipal/forest) permissions
- Specific insurance requirements
- Project-specific defect liability durations (often **12 to 36 months** for hardscape, **up to 60 months** for trees)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** If a question says *"Distinguish between GCC and SCC"*, the cleanest one-line answer is: *"GCC is the standard rulebook; SCC is the project's custom rulebook that overrides GCC where they conflict."*

### 2.4 Specifications — The Quality DNA

**Specifications** are written descriptions of the **quality, workmanship, and materials** to be used. They are of three main types:

| Type | Description | When Used in Landscaping |
|---|---|---|
| **General Specifications** | Broad material/workmanship standards (e.g., IS 14211 for tree saplings) | Whole project |
| **Detailed Specifications** | Item-by-item, full description (e.g., "Excavation in ordinary soil, depth 60 cm, disposing within 50 m") | BOQ-line items |
| **Performance Specifications** | Defines the **end result**, not the method (e.g., "lawn must achieve 95% green cover within 60 days") | Design-Build, EPC, Maintenance |

### 2.5 Bill of Quantities (BOQ) — The Commercial Spine

The BOQ is a **priced document** listing every measurable work item, its quantity, rate, and total amount. In landscape projects, a typical BOQ has:

- **Earthwork** (excavation, filling, grading) — measured in cum or sq.m
- **Horticulture** (plant supply, planting, lawn) — measured per no., per sq.m
- **Hardscape** (paving, kerbs, walls) — measured in sq.m, cum, Rmt
- **Irrigation** (pipes, sprinklers, valves) — measured in Rmt, per no.
- **Lighting** (cable, fixtures) — measured in Rmt, per no.
- **Maintenance** (per month or per visit) — lumpsum or per sq.m

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **BOQ is the basis of tender evaluation, payment certificates, and variation valuation**. A single missing item can cause major contractual disputes.

### 2.6 Significant Clauses — Defects, Maintenance, Arbitration

#### 2.6.1 Defects Liability Period (DLP)

- Period after practical completion during which the **contractor must rectify defects free of cost**.
- Landscape standard: **6 to 24 months** for hardscape, **up to 60 months** for plantation survival.
- Backed by a **Performance Security / Retention Money** (typically **5–10% of contract value**).

#### 2.6.2 Maintenance Clause

- Defines the contractor's obligation to **maintain the landscape** post-handover.
- Includes watering, weeding, pruning, pest control, replacement of dead plants.
- Specified frequency (daily, weekly, monthly) and reporting.

#### 2.6.3 Arbitration Clause

- Defines the mechanism to resolve disputes **outside court**.
- Typical structure: **Negotiation → Conciliation → Arbitration → (only if needed) Litigation**.
- Arbitration governed by the **Indian Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996** (as amended).
- Specifies: number of arbitrators (usually 1 or 3), seat/venue, language, governing law.

> [!WARNING]
> Under the **2015 and 2019 amendments**, the seat of arbitration is sacrosanct. KTU students must remember that *venue* (where hearings happen) ≠ *seat* (which determines the courts with supervisory jurisdiction).

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Point | Typical Value / Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Lump Sum Contract | Single fixed price | Best for defined scope |
| Cost Plus | Actual cost + fee % | 10–15% overhead + 8–12% profit |
| Unit Rate | Rate × Quantity | Most common in landscape tenders |
| GCC | Standard rulebook | CPWD / FIDIC / NIT |
| SCC | Project-specific overrides | Always read together with GCC |
| Specifications | Quality definition | IS Codes, MORTH, NHAI |
| BOQ | Paced document | Foundation of payment |
| Defects Liability Period | Post-completion defect window | 6–60 months |
| Retention Money | Security for defect rectification | 5–10% of contract value |
| Maintenance | Post-handover care | 12–36 months typical |
| Arbitration | Out-of-court dispute resolution | Arbitration Act 1996 (amended 2015, 2019) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Numerical Derivations

> [!NOTE]
> Although contract documentation is largely qualitative, the **BOQ** involves rigorous numerical logic. The most important calculation a landscape engineer performs is the **Rate Analysis** — determining the unit rate of a BOQ item. We derive this step by step.

### 3.1 Derivation: Unit Rate of a BOQ Item (e.g., Supply and Planting of a Tree)

The **unit rate** of a landscape BOQ item is built from the sum of **(a) Material Cost, (b) Labour Cost, (c) Equipment Cost, (d) Overhead, (e) Profit**.

**Step 1 — Identify the components of the item.**
For *"Supply, planting, and 1-year maintenance of one 2.0 m tall Ficus benjamina tree"*:

- Material: sapling, manure, fertilizer, pesticide, staking material, water
- Labour: digging pit, planting, watering, weeding
- Equipment: transportation, tools
- Overheads: site supervision, office
- Profit: contractor's margin

**Step 2 — Compute the Material Cost.**

$$C_m = \sum (Q_i \times P_i)$$

where $Q_i$ = quantity of material $i$ per tree, $P_i$ = unit price of material $i$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Labour Cost.**

$$C_l = \sum (H_j \times W_j)$$

where $H_j$ = hours of labour $j$ per tree, $W_j$ = hourly wage of labour $j$.

**Step 4 — Compute the Equipment Cost.**

$$C_e = \sum (T_k \times R_k)$$

where $T_k$ = time equipment $k$ is used per tree (hours), $R_k$ = hourly rate of equipment $k$.

**Step 5 — Compute the Overhead and Profit.**

$$C_{oh} = p_1 \times (C_m + C_l + C_e)$$
$$C_p = p_2 \times (C_m + C_l + C_e + C_{oh})$$

where $p_1$ = overhead percentage (typically **10–15%**), $p_2$ = profit percentage (typically **8–12%**).

**Step 6 — Total Unit Rate.**

$$R = C_m + C_l + C_e + C_{oh} + C_p$$

**Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example.**

Consider the same tree item with the following data:

| Component | Quantity | Rate (₹) | Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2.0 m Ficus sapling | 1 no. | 450 | 450 |
| Organic manure (5 kg) | 5 kg | 12/kg | 60 |
| Staking (2 bamboo + rope) | 1 set | 35 | 35 |
| Labour (digging 0.6 m pit, planting, staking) | 2 hrs | 80/hr | 160 |
| Labour (watering & weeding, 30 visits × 0.5 hr) | 15 hrs | 80/hr | 1,200 |
| Transportation (shared with 50 trees) | 0.5 hr | 200/hr | 100 |
| Water charges | 1,000 L | 0.10/L | 100 |

Summing direct costs:

$$C_m + C_l + C_e = 450 + 60 + 35 + 160 + 1{,}200 + 100 + 100 = 2{,}105 \text{ ₹}$$

Add overhead at 12%:

$$C_{oh} = 0.12 \times 2{,}105 = 252.60 \text{ ₹}$$

Add profit at 10% on (direct + overhead):

$$C_p = 0.10 \times (2{,}105 + 252.60) = 235.76 \text{ ₹}$$

Final unit rate:

$$R = 2{,}105 + 252.60 + 235.76 = 2{,}593.36 \approx 2{,}593 \text{ ₹ per tree}$$

**Step 8 — Extend to BOQ Total.**
If the project needs **120 such trees**, the BOQ line total is:

$$\text{BOQ Line Total} = 120 \times 2{,}593 = 3{,}11{,}160 \text{ ₹}$$

This single number flows into the **Schedule of Rates, the Tender, the Payment Certificates, and the Variation Valuations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked *"How is the unit rate of a BOQ item determined?"*, the answer is the 6-step logical breakdown above: Material + Labour + Equipment + Overhead + Profit. The numerical example above is the kind of working the examiner expects.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Which Form of Contract for Which Landscape Project?

| Landscape Project Type | Recommended Form of Contract | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Defined urban plaza (drawings complete) | **Lump Sum** | Scope is fixed and measurable |
| Highway median plantation (length uncertain) | **Unit Rate** | Quantities are measured progressively |
| Ecological restoration (high surprise) | **Cost Plus** | Hidden risks (e.g., contaminated soil) |
| Integrated resort landscape (design + build) | **Turnkey / Design-Build** | Single-point accountability |
| Annual maintenance of existing garden | **Lump Sum or Unit Rate** | Recurring scope with measured visits |

### 3.3 Defects Liability Period — Decision Logic

The DLP is **not arbitrary**. It is tied to the *establishment period* of the planted material:

- Turf/lawn → **3 to 6 months** (to verify root establishment)
- Shrubs and ground cover → **6 to 12 months**
- Small trees (2–3 m) → **12 to 24 months**
- Large trees (> 5 m) → **36 to 60 months**
- Hardscape (paving, walls) → **12 to 24 months**
- Irrigation network → **12 to 24 months**

> [!WARNING]
> KTU students often quote a single DLP for a whole project. The correct approach is to **state multiple DLPs for different categories** of work within the same contract.

### 3.4 Arbitration Clause — Logical Sequence

The standard Indian contract follows this dispute resolution ladder:

1. **Engineer / Employer's Representative** reviews the claim.
2. **Amicable settlement** through negotiation (typically 28–56 days).
3. **Conciliation** by an independent conciliator.
4. **Arbitration** by a sole arbitrator (or a 3-member tribunal) under the **Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996**.
5. **Court intervention** only for setting aside or enforcement of the award — not re-trial.

The **seat of arbitration** (e.g., "the seat shall be Thiruvananthapuram") is the legal anchor that determines which court supervises the process.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Contract Documentation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CONTRACT DOCUMENT] --> B[FORM OF CONTRACT]
    A --> C[GENERAL CONDITIONS OF CONTRACT]
    A --> D[SPECIAL CONDITIONS OF CONTRACT]
    A --> E[SPECIFICATIONS]
    A --> F[BILL OF QUANTITIES]
    A --> G[DRAWINGS]
    A --> H[ANNEXURES AND APPENDICES]

    B --> B1[Lump Sum]
    B --> B2[Unit Rate]
    B --> B3[Cost Plus]
    B --> B4[Turnkey Design Build]

    C --> C1[Definitions]
    C --> C2[Obligations]
    C --> C3[Payment Terms]
    C --> C4[Delays and Damages]
    C --> C5[Force Majeure]

    D --> D1[Project Specific Overrides]
    D --> D2[Local Conditions]
    D --> D3[Special Materials]

    E --> E1[General Specifications]
    E --> E2[Detailed Specifications]
    E --> E3[Performance Specifications]

    F --> F1[Earthwork]
    F --> F2[Horticulture]
    F --> F3[Hardscape]
    F --> F4[Irrigation]
    F --> F5[Lighting]
    F --> F6[Maintenance]

    H --> H1[Arbitration Clause]
    H --> H2[Defects Liability Clause]
    H --> H3[Maintenance Clause]
    H --> H4[Performance Security]
```

### 4.2 BOQ Preparation and Rate Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Site Survey and Measurement] --> P2[Prepare Unpriced BOQ]
    P2 --> P3[Identify Resources per Item]
    P3 --> P4[Material Cost]
    P3 --> P5[Labour Cost]
    P3 --> P6[Equipment Cost]
    P4 --> P7[Direct Cost Sum]
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Add Overhead]
    P8 --> P9[Add Profit]
    P9 --> P10[Unit Rate]
    P10 --> P11[Multiply by Quantity]
    P11 --> P12[BOQ Line Total]
    P12 --> P13[Tender Submission and Evaluation]
    P13 --> P14[Payment Certificates]
    P14 --> P15[Variation Valuations]
```

### 4.3 Dispute Resolution and Arbitration Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Dispute Arises] --> S2[Engineer or Employer Representative Review]
    S2 --> S3{Resolved in 28 days?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Closure]
    S3 -- No --> S5[Amicable Negotiation]
    S5 --> S6{Resolved in 56 days?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S4
    S6 -- No --> S7[Conciliation]
    S7 --> S8{Resolved?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S4
    S8 -- No --> S9[Arbitration Tribunal under Act 1996]
    S9 --> S10[Arbitral Award]
    S10 --> S11[Enforcement or Setting Aside by Court]
```

### 4.4 Defects Liability Period Logic by Landscape Element

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Landscape Element] --> L1[Turf and Lawn]
    L --> L2[Shrubs and Ground Cover]
    L --> L3[Small Trees 2 to 3 m]
    L --> L4[Large Trees above 5 m]
    L --> L5[Hardscape]
    L --> L6[Irrigation Network]

    L1 --> D1[DLP 3 to 6 months]
    L2 --> D2[DLP 6 to 12 months]
    L3 --> D3[DLP 12 to 24 months]
    L4 --> D4[DLP 36 to 60 months]
    L5 --> D5[DLP 12 to 24 months]
    L6 --> D6[DLP 12 to 24 months]
```

### 4.5 Comparison Block — GCC vs SCC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[GCC General Conditions] --> G1[Standard across all projects of an organization]
    G --> G2[Pre printed in tender booklet]
    G --> G3[Cannot be negotiated by bidder]

    S[SCC Special Conditions] --> S1[Project specific]
    S --> S2[Drafted per project]
    S --> S3[Can override GCC clauses]
    S --> S4[Read together with GCC]

    G --> X[Conflict Resolution]
    S --> X
    X --> R[SCC prevails over GCC where they conflict]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
> *Define a "Lump Sum Contract" and state one situation in a landscape project where it is most suitable.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Lump Sum Contract is a form of contract in which the contractor agrees to execute the entire defined scope of work for a single, fixed total price, irrespective of the actual quantities executed. *(1.5 marks)* It shifts the quantity risk to the contractor. It is most suitable for **clearly defined landscape projects with completed drawings**, such as an urban rooftop garden, a defined-entry plaza, or a finished-design sensory park where scope changes are minimal. *(1.5 marks)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
> *What is the Bill of Quantities (BOQ) and why is it called the "commercial spine" of a landscape contract?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The BOQ is a priced document listing every measurable work item, its quantity, unit rate, and total amount, prepared from the drawings and specifications. *(1 mark)* It is called the "commercial spine" because it is the basis for: (i) tendering and bid evaluation, (ii) interim and final payment certificates, (iii) valuation of variations, and (iv) final account settlement. *(2 marks)* Without a proper BOQ, no payment or variation can be objectively measured in monetary terms.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** — Module 2 Choice Question
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **four major forms of contract** used in landscape projects, with one suitable example for each. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Differentiate between **General Conditions of Contract (GCC)** and **Special Conditions of Contract (SCC)**. Why must the two always be read together? *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Four Forms of Contract (7 marks):**

1. **Lump Sum Contract** — A single fixed total price for the whole defined scope. Risk on contractor. *Example:* Construction of a 1-acre corporate garden with hardscape and softscape, drawings fully prepared; contractor quoted ₹85 lakhs all-inclusive. *(1.75 marks)*

2. **Unit Rate (Item Rate) Contract** — Contractor quotes rates per unit (per sq.m of lawn, per tree); quantities are measured from the BOQ. Risk shared; best when quantities can be reliably measured. *Example:* Highway median plantation of 12 km — contractor quotes ₹650 per tree including 1-year maintenance. *(1.75 marks)*

3. **Cost Plus Contract** — Contractor reimbursed actual cost plus an agreed fee (percentage or fixed). Best for undefined, evolving, high-surprise work. *Example:* Ecological restoration of a degraded wetland where buried debris and soil contamination can only be discovered on excavation. *(1.75 marks)*

4. **Turnkey / Design-Build Contract** — Single contractor responsible for both design and execution. Best for integrated scope. *Example:* An IT campus landscape with irrigation, lighting, hardscape, and plantation delivered as one integrated package. *(1.75 marks)*

**(b) GCC vs SCC (7 marks):**

| Basis | GCC | SCC |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Standard rulebook applicable to all contracts of an organization | Project-specific rules that override GCC |
| Source | Pre-printed in tender documents | Drafted for the specific project |
| Scope | Broad and general (definitions, obligations, payment, delays, arbitration) | Project-specific (site conditions, plant species, climatic constraints) |
| Negotiability | Usually non-negotiable | Often the most negotiated section |
| Legal weight | Acts as default law of the contract | Prevails over GCC where they conflict |

*(5 marks for the differentiation and table)*

**Why they must be read together (2 marks):**
GCC provides the universal legal framework, but every landscape project has unique conditions (climate, soil, species, permissions, working hours). SCC customizes GCC for those specific conditions. Reading them in isolation leads to misinterpretation. The cardinal rule is: **"Read GCC first, then apply SCC to amend, supplement, or override any clause."**

---

> **Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** — Alternative Choice
>
> **(a)** What are **Specifications** in a landscape contract? Explain the three types with examples. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the significant clauses pertaining to **(i) Defects Liability, (ii) Maintenance, and (iii) Arbitration** as applicable to landscape projects. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Specifications (7 marks):**

Specifications are written descriptions of the **quality, materials, workmanship, and performance standards** of the work to be executed. They are the *quality DNA* of the contract.

- **General Specifications (2 marks):** Broad, project-wide standards citing Indian Standard codes. *Example:* "All tree saplings shall conform to IS 14211:2018 with a healthy, well-formed crown and a straight trunk of minimum 2.0 m height."

- **Detailed Specifications (2.5 marks):** Item-by-item, method-and-material level description tied to BOQ line items. *Example:* "Excavation of tree pits 0.6 m × 0.6 m × 0.6 m in ordinary soil, including disposal of surplus earth within 50 m lead, refilling with a 1:1:1 mix of garden soil, sand, and FYM, and light watering."

- **Performance Specifications (2.5 marks):** Defines the **end result**, leaving the method to the contractor. *Example:* "The contractor shall achieve and maintain a minimum 95% green cover of specified turf grass species within 60 days of sowing, with no patches exceeding 0.5 sq.m at the end of the establishment period."

**(b) Significant Clauses (7 marks):**

**(i) Defects Liability (2.5 marks):**
The Defects Liability Period (DLP) is the time after practical completion during which the contractor must rectify any defect, shrinkage, or failure **free of cost**. For landscape, DLPs are stratified:
- Turf: 3–6 months
- Shrubs: 6–12 months
- Trees: 12–60 months (depending on size)
- Hardscape: 12–24 months
Secured by **Retention Money / Performance Security** of 5–10% of contract value.

**(ii) Maintenance (2.5 marks):**
The maintenance clause obligates the contractor to care for the landscape post-handover, including watering, weeding, pruning, fertilizer application, pest control, and replacement of dead plants. Frequency, reporting, and a measurable performance benchmark (e.g., "95% plant survival at end of maintenance period") must be clearly defined.

**(iii) Arbitration (2 marks):**
A pre-agreed mechanism to resolve disputes outside court. The standard ladder is **Engineer → Negotiation → Conciliation → Arbitration under the Indian Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 (as amended in 2015 and 2019)**. Specifies number of arbitrators, **seat** (legally binding), language, and governing law. Court intervention is limited to enforcement or setting-aside of the award.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "venue" and "seat" of arbitration** — venue is only the physical location of hearings; *seat* determines the courts with supervisory jurisdiction. Examiners specifically test this distinction. *(−2 marks if wrong)*
> 2. **Quoting a single DLP for the whole contract** — examiners expect *category-wise* DLPs (turf, shrubs, trees, hardscape).
> 3. **Calling Specifications and BOQ the same thing** — Specifications describe *quality*, BOQ describes *quantity and cost*. A common but costly confusion.
> 4. **Forgetting retention money/security in the defects clause** — without it, the defects clause is unenforceable in practice.
> 5. **Writing "Cost Plus" and "Lump Sum" interchangeably** — these are opposites. Cost Plus pays actual cost plus fee; Lump Sum pays a single fixed total irrespective of actual cost.
> 6. **Missing the Act reference in arbitration** — the examiner expects "Arbitration and Conciliation Act, **1996** (as **amended in 2015 and 2019**)". A bare mention of "1996" loses marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Contract Documentation** = Form + GCC + SCC + Specifications + BOQ + Annexures (Arbitration, DLP, Maintenance, Security).
- **Four Forms of Contract:** Lump Sum, Unit Rate, Cost Plus, Turnkey — each allocates risk differently.
- **GCC** = standard, non-negotiable, project-wide; **SCC** = project-specific, overrides GCC where they conflict.
- **Specifications** come in three flavours: General, Detailed, Performance — pick the right one for the BOQ line.
- **BOQ** is the commercial spine: it underpins tendering, payment, variations, and final accounts.
- **Unit Rate** of any BOQ item = Material + Labour + Equipment + Overhead % + Profit %.
- **Defects Liability Period (DLP)** is *not* uniform — it varies by element (turf 3–6 months, trees up to 60 months).
- **Maintenance Clause** must specify frequency, scope, and measurable success criteria (e.g., 95% survival).
- **Arbitration** follows the ladder: Engineer → Negotiation → Conciliation → Arbitration under the **Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 (amended 2015, 2019)**.
- **Seat** of arbitration (not venue) determines the supervisory court.
- **Retention Money / Performance Security** (5–10%) is the financial guarantee backing the DLP.
- **Living materials = unique risk** in landscape contracts — make sure your documentation explicitly addresses plant death, replacement, and survival guarantees.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parties to the Contract: Their Roles, Contractual Relationships and Legal Obligations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT612 - LANDSCAPE DESIGN AND MANGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parties to the Contract: Their Roles, Contractual Relationships and Legal Obligations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Contract Definition (Indian Contract Act, 1872 — Section 2(h)):** A contract is a legally enforceable agreement between two or more parties, made with the free consent of parties competent to contract, for a lawful consideration and with a lawful object.

In the context of **Landscape Design and Management**, every landscape project — whether a private garden, a public park, a campus landscape, or a township greenbelt — is executed through a **multi-party contract framework**. The *Parties to the Contract* refers to the distinct legal and professional entities that enter into binding agreements to design, execute, supervise, supply, and finance the landscape development works.

The standard contractual hierarchy in landscape projects typically includes:

| Sl. No | Party | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Owner / Employer / Client** | Commissioning & financing party |
| 2 | **Landscape Architect / Consultant** | Design & conceptual authority |
| 3 | **Contractor (Main / Prime)** | Execution of landscape works |
| 4 | **Project Manager / Engineer** | Coordination & supervision |
| 5 | **Sub-Contractor** | Specialist works (irrigation, lighting, etc.) |
| 6 | **Suppliers / Vendors** | Material & plant supply |
| 7 | **Surety / Banker / Insurer** | Performance guarantee & risk cover |
| 8 | **Statutory Authorities** | Legal approvals (Panchayat/Municipality) |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy: A Wedding Ceremony**  
> Imagine organizing a large wedding:  
> - The **Owner** = The family hosting the wedding (pays for it, decides the budget)  
> - The **Landscape Architect / Designer** = The wedding planner who designs the mandap & stage layout  
> - The **Contractor** = The decorator and event crew who *build* the arrangements  
> - The **Sub-Contractor** = The florist or caterer hired for one specific job  
> - The **Project Manager** = The coordinator who ensures the planner's design is followed on the day  
> - The **Supplier** = The shop delivering flowers, fabric, props  
> - The **Banker / Surety** = The family friend who guarantees expenses will be paid  

In landscape projects, each party has a **specific role**, a **specific contract** with specific others, and **specific legal duties**. If one party fails (e.g., the florist doesn't deliver flowers), the entire chain suffers. Hence, the law creates *privity of contract* — only the directly contracted parties can sue each other.

---

### 1.3 Key Legal Foundations

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Statutory Backbone Governing Landscape Contracts in India:**
> - **Indian Contract Act, 1872** — Defines valid contract, obligations, breach, damages  
> - **Sale of Goods Act, 1930** — Governs supply of plants & materials  
> - **Indian Easements Act, 1882** — Rights over adjoining land  
> - **Consumer Protection Act, 2019** — Liability for defective service  
> - **Specific Relief Act, 1963** — Enforcement of contracts  
> - **Indian Forest Act & State Tree Protection Acts** — Plant sourcing & transplantation laws  
> - **Town & Country Planning Acts / Kerala Municipality Act** — Statutory approvals  
> - **Indian Stamp Act** — Stamp duty on agreements  
> - **Arbitration & Conciliation Act, 1996** — Dispute resolution

### 1.4 Standard Physical / Financial Constants in Landscape Contracting

> [!NOTE]  
> - **Earnest Money Deposit (EMD)** = typically **2% to 5%** of contract value  
> - **Security Deposit / Performance Guarantee** = **5% to 10%** of contract value  
> - **Retention Money** = **5% to 10%** held back from RA Bills  
> - **Defect Liability Period (DLP)** for softscape (plants) = **12 to 24 months**  
> - **Defect Liability Period (DLP)** for hardscape (paths, structures) = **12 to 36 months**  
> - **Mobilization Advance** = up to **10%** of contract value  
> - **Liquidated Damages** = typically capped at **10%** of contract value  

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & Roles of Each Party

### 2.1 The Owner / Employer / Client

**Role:** The Owner is the **initiating and financing party** — the person or body for whom the landscape is designed and built. In KTU context, the Owner may be:
- A private individual (villa owner)
- A developer (real-estate township)
- A public body (Municipality, Smart City Mission, PWD)
- An institution (KTU campus, hospital, school)

**Rights of the Owner:**
- To receive the landscape as per design and specification
- To inspect progress at any reasonable time
- To terminate the contract for default (with notice)
- To withhold payment for non-conforming work
- To claim liquidated damages for delay

**Duties / Legal Obligations of the Owner:**
- To provide **clear, unencumbered site possession**
- To make **payments on certified bills** within the agreed period (usually 30 days)
- To appoint a competent **Landscape Architect / PMC**
- To obtain **statutory approvals** at his cost (building permits, tree-cutting permission)
- To provide accurate **topographical and soil data**
- To **not interfere** with the contractor's independent execution

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Legal Doctrine — "Prevention Principle":** If the Owner causes delay (e.g., late site handover), the contractor is entitled to **extension of time (EoT)** and **compensation for idle resources**. This is settled under *Indian Contract Act Section 73 (Compensation for breach)*.

---

### 2.2 The Landscape Architect / Consultant / Designer

**Role:** The professional who conceives, plans, and details the landscape — softscape (plants, lawn, water bodies) and hardscape (paths, pavilions, lighting, irrigation).

**Rights:**
- Fee for design services (often a percentage of project cost, typically **8%–15%**)
- Copyright over design drawings
- To supervise execution and reject non-conforming work
- To issue variation orders within scope

**Duties / Legal Obligations:**
- **Duty of Care & Skill** — standard of a reasonably competent professional
- **Duty to Warn** — inform owner of design risks (e.g., a tree species unsuitable for soil)
- **Duty to Coordinate** — issue instructions consistently to contractor
- **Duty not to vary scope unilaterally** without written owner's approval
- Maintain professional indemnity insurance

> [!WARNING]  
> The Landscape Architect owes a **tortious duty of care** to third parties (e.g., users of the park). Defective design causing injury attracts liability under *Consumer Protection Act 2019* and *Law of Torts*.

---

### 2.3 The Contractor (Main / Prime Contractor)

**Role:** The executing agency — converts design into physical reality through labour, materials, and plant stock.

**Types of Landscape Contractors:**
- **General Contractor** — overall responsibility
- **Specialist Contractor** — only turfing, only irrigation, only lighting

**Rights of Contractor:**
- Right to **receive timely payment** for certified work
- Right to **Extension of Time (EoT)** for genuine delays
- Right to **suspend work** for non-payment (after notice)
- Right to claim **extra items / variations** at agreed rates
- Right to **arbitration** in case of disputes

**Duties / Legal Obligations:**
- Execute work as per drawings, BOQ, and specifications
- Comply with statutory laws (labour, environment, tree-cutting)
- Provide **plants of specified girth, height, and species**
- Maintain site safety and indemnify the owner
- Submit **Running Account (RA) Bills** for progress payments
- Rectify defects during DLP **without extra cost**
- Arrange insurance and workman compensation
