# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define "Hydrozoning" in landscape irrigation and list its three benefits.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Hydrozoning is the practice of grouping plants with similar water requirements (high, medium, low) onto the same irrigation valve/zone.

**Three Benefits (2 marks):**
1. **Water conservation** — avoids over-watering drought-tolerant species.
2. **Plant health** — prevents fungal disease from over-irrigation.
3. **Cost efficiency** — reduces pump run-time and energy bills.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Manning's equation and explain the significance of the roughness coefficient "n" in drainage pipe design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Equation (1.5 marks):**
$$V = \frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

**Significance of n (1.5 marks):** The roughness coefficient "n" represents the internal surface friction of the pipe. Typical values: **PVC = 0.010–0.013**, **concrete = 0.013–0.015**, **corrugated metal = 0.022–0.024**. A higher n means more friction loss, lower velocity, and greater chance of siltation — therefore PVC is preferred in landscape drainage.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Design a drip irrigation system for a 2000 m² residential park in Calicut. The area has the following planting:**
- 60% Lawn ($K_c = 0.8$)
- 25% Shrubs ($K_c = 0.6$)
- 15% Trees ($K_c = 0.5$)

Reference ET₀ = 5.0 mm/day. System efficiency = 90% (drip). Daily irrigation window = 5 hours.

**Sub-parts:**

**(a) (7 marks)** Calculate the total daily water demand in litres and the design pump discharge in L/s.

**(b) (7 marks)** Sketch the typical layout of the drip system showing mainline, sub-main, zone valves, laterals, and emitters.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Area-wise distribution**

$$A_{lawn} = 2000 \times 0.60 = 1200 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{shrubs} = 2000 \times 0.25 = 500 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{trees} = 2000 \times 0.15 = 300 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Net PWR for each zone**

$$PWR_{lawn} = 5.0 \times 0.8 = 4.0 \text{ mm/day}$$

$$PWR_{shrubs} = 5.0 \times 0.6 = 3.0 \text{ mm/day}$$

$$PWR_{trees} = 5.0 \times 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ mm/day}$$

**Step 3 — Gross PWR (dividing by 0.9 efficiency)**

$$PWR_{lawn,gross} = 4.0 / 0.9 = 4.44 \text{ mm/day}$$

$$PWR_{shrubs,gross} = 3.0 / 0.9 = 3.33 \text{ mm/day}$$

$$PWR_{trees,gross} = 2.5 / 0.9 = 2.78 \text{ mm/day}$$

**Step 4 — Volume per zone (1 mm = 1 L/m²)**

$$V_{lawn} = 4.44 \times 1200 = 5328 \text{ L/day}$$

$$V_{shrubs} = 3.33 \times 500 = 1665 \text{ L/day}$$

$$V_{trees} = 2.78 \times 300 = 834 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 5 — Total Daily Demand**

$$V_{total} = 5328 + 1665 + 834 = 7827 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 6 — Peak Pump Discharge (worst case = lawn zone running)**

$$Q_{pump} = \frac{V_{lawn}}{t} = \frac{5328}{5 \times 3600} = \frac{5328}{18000} = 0.296 \text{ L/s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{pump} \approx 0.30 \text{ L/s} \; (\approx 1.07 \text{ m}^3\text{/hr})}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

The layout (already depicted in Section 4.2) should include:

1. **Source** → Pump → Filter → Fertigation tank.
2. **Mainline** 50 mm PVC class-2 from pump to garden entry.
3. **Three Zone Valves** (1" solenoid) — one each for lawn, shrubs, trees.
4. **Sub-mains** 40 mm PE running along each zone.
5. **Laterals** 16 mm PE for shrubs (emitters 0.30 m apart, 4 L/hr).
6. **Drip tubes** 20 mm for trees (2 bubblers of 8 L/hr each).
7. **Controller** with rain-sensor feedback.
8. **Pressure gauge** and **flushing valve** at end of each lateral.

**Components list for full marks (7 marks):**
- Source connection (0.5)
- Pump + filter + fertigation (1)
- Mainline + sub-main sizes (1)
- Three zone valves + controller (1)
- Lateral + emitter specification (1)
- Head-to-head coverage and flushing points (1)
- Cross-section detail of emitter (0.5)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Design the stormwater drainage for a 1.0 ha landscape campus with 50% lawn, 30% paved, and 20% roof area. Design rainfall intensity = 90 mm/hr. Pipe slope = 1.5%. Manning's n = 0.013.**

**(a) (7 marks)** Calculate the peak runoff using the Rational Method.

**(b) (7 marks)** Determine the required diameter of a PVC pipe running full using Manning's equation and verify the self-cleansing velocity.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute individual areas**

$$A_{lawn} = 0.50 \text{ ha}, \quad A_{paved} = 0.30 \text{ ha}, \quad A_{roof} = 0.20 \text{ ha}$$

**Step 2 — Runoff coefficients (standard values)**

$$C_{lawn} = 0.35, \quad C_{paved} = 0.90, \quad C_{roof} = 0.95$$

**Step 3 — Composite C**

$$C = \frac{(0.35 \times 0.50) + (0.90 \times 0.30) + (0.95 \times 0.20)}{1.00}$$

$$C = \frac{0.175 + 0.270 + 0.190}{1.00} = 0.635$$

**Step 4 — Apply Rational Method**

$$Q = C \cdot I \cdot A = 0.635 \times 90 \times 1.0 = 57.15 \text{ L/s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{peak} = 57.15 \text{ L/s} = 0.0572 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

For a circular pipe flowing full, $A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$, $R = \frac{d}{4}$, $S = 0.015$.

Manning's equation:

$$Q = A \cdot \frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

$$0.0572 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{0.013} \cdot \left(\frac{d}{4}\right)^{2/3} \cdot (0.015)^{1/2}$$

$$0.0572 = \frac{\pi}{0.052} \cdot d^2 \cdot d^{2/3} \cdot 4^{-2/3} \cdot 0.1225$$

$$0.0572 = 60.36 \cdot d^{8/3} \cdot 0.3968 \cdot 0.1225$$

$$0.0572 = 2.934 \cdot d^{8/3}$$

$$d^{8/3} = \frac{0.0572}{2.934} = 0.0195$$

$$d = (0.0195)^{0.375} = 0.169 \text{ m} \approx 169 \text{ mm}$$

**Select commercial size: 200 mm (8") PVC SN-4 pipe.**

**Velocity Check:**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.0572}{(\pi/4)(0.20)^2} = \frac{0.0572}{0.0314} = 1.82 \text{ m/s}$$

**Check: 1.82 m/s > 0.75 m/s (self-cleansing) and < 3.0 m/s (max non-scouring) → ACCEPTABLE**

$$\boxed{\text{Use 200 mm PVC pipe at 1.5\% slope}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Utility Design Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in single-phase voltage-drop calculations (−1 mark).
> 2. **Using net PWR instead of gross PWR** (ignoring system efficiency) — examiners deduct 1.5 marks.
> 3. **Not converting mm/day to L/day** before dividing by time — leads to wrong pump size.
> 4. **Selecting pipe sizes without velocity check** — must verify 0.75 ≤ V ≤ 3.0 m/s.
> 5. **Mixing up C values** — concrete roof runoff is 0.95, not 0.85.
> 6. **Not specifying IP rating** for outdoor electrical fittings — must state **IP65 minimum**.
> 7. **Omitting earthing** in single-line diagrams — full 2 marks lost.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Irrigation design** is a 4-step process: PWR → TWD → System selection → Hydraulic sizing.
- **Hydrozoning** is the single most important water-saving principle.
- **Rational Method (Q = C·I·A)** is the KTU favourite for stormwater design.
- **Manning's equation** applies to gravity flow; **Hazen-Williams** applies to pressurised irrigation laterals.
- **Drip efficiency = 90%**, **Sprinkler = 75%**, **Rotor = 70%** — remember these for sizing.
- **Drainage gradient:** minimum **1% for landscape, 2% for paved** to avoid ponding.
- **Self-cleansing velocity** must exceed **0.75 m/s**; **max velocity** should not exceed **3 m/s** to prevent pipe scour.
- **Voltage drop** in landscape lighting must be **≤ 4%** of supply voltage.
- **Outdoor electrical fittings** require a minimum **IP65** rating; submersible need **IP67**.
- **RCD sensitivity** for outdoor human protection = **30 mA**.
- **ELV (12 V)** is preferred for all landscape lighting — no electrical licence needed in India for < 50 V installations.
- **Earthing resistance** must be **≤ 5 Ω** (IS 3043) for any landscape installation.
- **Pipe materials:** PVC class-2 for irrigation mains; PE 80/100 for drip laterals; RCC NP3/NP4 for stormwater outfalls.
- **Always coordinate utility plans with grading and planting drawings** to avoid trench clashes with tree roots.
- **Catch basin spacing** rule of thumb: **30 m apart in paved areas, 50 m in lawns.**
- **KTU frequently asks:** "Compare drip and sprinkler" — remember: drip = high efficiency, low flow, no wind drift; sprinkler = faster, covers turf, higher evaporation loss.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods and Materials Involved in Maintenance of the Landscape Features, Maintenance Bids and Services Involved
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT612 - LANDSCAPE DESIGN AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Methods and Materials Involved in Maintenance of the Landscape Features, Maintenance Bids and Services Involved

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Landscape Maintenance** is the systematic, scheduled, and technically executed set of cultural, mechanical, chemical, and biological operations performed upon a designed landscape to preserve its aesthetic value, functional utility, ecological stability, and design intent throughout its intended life-cycle.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, landscape maintenance is classified under **post-occupancy landscape management** and forms the operational continuation of the design-implementation-maintenance triad. It encompasses all horticultural practices applied to **softscape** (living elements like turf, trees, shrubs, ground covers, annuals) and **hard landscape elements** (pavers, walls, lighting, water bodies, irrigation networks).

### Sub-Definition: Maintenance Bids and Services

A **Maintenance Bid** is a formal, legally binding commercial offer submitted by a landscape contractor specifying the **scope, schedule, materials, labor, equipment, and cost** for executing specified maintenance operations over a defined contract period (typically 1–3 years for institutional landscapes).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OEAGT612, Module 3):**
> The course explicitly requires students to identify appropriate **methods**, **materials**, **service categories**, and **bidding procedures** involved in maintaining landscape features. This integrates **horticulture, civil engineering, and contract management** domains.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a landscape like a **living piece of machinery** — say, a car. Once manufactured (planted/installed), it cannot just be left alone. It needs:

| Car Analogy | Landscape Equivalent |
|-------------|----------------------|
| Engine oil change | Soil enrichment, fertilization |
| Tire rotation | Lawn aeration, mowing |
| Paint polishing | Pruning, mulching |
| Mechanic service | Landscape contractor's maintenance visit |
| Service center quotation | Maintenance bid document |

Just as a car owner gets **periodic service quotes from garages**, a property owner solicits **maintenance bids from landscape contractors**. The garage's quote lists what they will do (oil change, brake check), what parts they will use (brand X oil, OEM filters), how often (every 5,000 km), and the total cost — exactly mirroring a landscape maintenance bid.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Operational Metrics in Landscape Maintenance:**
> - **Mowing Height (Turfgrass):** **25–50 mm** for cool-season grasses; **15–25 mm** for warm-season grasses
> - **Mowing Frequency:** Based on **1/3 Rule** — never remove more than 1/3 of blade height per cut
> - **Pruning Window Frequency:** **2–3 times/year** for deciduous trees; **once/year** for conifers
> - **Mulch Depth:** **50–75 mm** (2–3 inches) standard
> - **Irrigation Depth:** **25–30 mm/week** for established turf (rainfall equivalent)
> - **Fertilizer N-P-K Ratios (Standard):** Turf 3:1:2 ratio; Trees 2:1:1 ratio
> - **Aeration Frequency:** **1–2 times/year** (spring/autumn)
> - **Bid Currency Unit:** INR per square meter per month (₹/m²/month) for institutional contracts

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Landscape Maintenance Operations

Landscape maintenance operations are classified into **five functional categories**, each with distinct methods and materials.

### A. Turfgrass (Lawn) Maintenance

#### Methods:
1. **Mowing** — Cutting grass blades to uniform height using reel or rotary mowers
2. **Edging** — Trimming borders along hardscape interfaces
3. **Fertilization** — Application of macro and micronutrients
4. **Irrigation** — Supplemental watering to meet evapotranspiration deficits
5. **Aeration** — Mechanical removal of soil cores to relieve compaction
6. **Dethatching** — Removal of excessive thatch (>12 mm layer)
7. **Topdressing** — Application of sand/soil mix to level surface
8. **Overseeding** — Reseeding bare/thin patches
9. **Weed Control** — Pre-emergent and post-emergent herbicide application
10. **Pest & Disease Management** — IPM (Integrated Pest Management) protocols

#### Materials:
- **Fertilizers:** Urea (46-0-0), DAP (18-46-0), MOP (0-0-60), NPK complexes
- **Herbicides:** Glyphosate (non-selective), 2,4-D (broadleaf selective), Atrazine (pre-emergent)
- **Seed:** Ryegrass, Bermuda, Fescue blends
- **Topdressing Material:** Sharp sand + compost (ratio 3:1)
- **Water:** pH 6.5–7.5 preferred for turf

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 1/3 Rule Formula:** If a turf species has an optimal mowing height H_opt, the maximum blade length before mowing should be:
> $$\text{Maximum Height Before Cut} = H_{opt} \times 1.33$$

---

### B. Tree and Shrub Maintenance

#### Methods:
1. **Pruning** — Selective removal of plant parts (branches, buds, roots)
2. **Crown Cleaning** — Removal of dead, diseased, crossing branches
3. **Crown Thinning** — Selective removal to increase light penetration
4. **Crown Raising/Lifting** — Removal of lower branches for clearance
5. **Crown Reduction** — Reducing overall size for utility clearance
6. **Watering** — Deep watering (15–20 L per tree per session)
7. **Mulching** — Organic/inorganic mulch application around root zone
8. **Staking & Guying** — Mechanical support for young trees
9. **Pest/Disease Control** — Spraying, trunk injection
10. **Fertilization** — Soil drench or foliar application

#### Materials:
- **Pruning Tools:** Secateurs, loppers, pole pruners, pruning saws, chainsaws
- **Disinfectants:** 10% bleach or 70% ethanol (for tool sterilization)
- **Wound Dressing:** Tree wound paint (bordeaux paste) — controversial, optional
- **Mulches:** Wood chips, bark, coco peat, leaf litter, gravel
- **Fertilizers:** Slow-release (osmocote type), organic (vermicompost, FYM)
- **Pesticides:** Neem oil, chlorpyriphos, imidacloprid

> [!NOTE]
> **Pruning Cuts Classification:**
> - **Heading Cut:** Removes apical dominance, encourages bushier regrowth (used for hedges)
> - **Thinning Cut:** Removes entire branch at origin, maintains natural form (used for shade trees)

---

### C. Hardscape Maintenance

#### Methods:
1. **Sweeping & Cleaning** — Regular removal of debris
2. **Pressure Washing** — Removal of algae, stains, dirt
3. **Joint Sand Replenishment** — Refilling paver joints
4. **Sealing** — Application of sealants to stone/concrete
5. **Repointing** — Repair of mortar joints in walls
6. **Crack Repair** — Epoxy injection or patching compounds
7. **Rust/Stain Removal** — Oxalic acid or commercial cleaners
8. **Lighting Maintenance** — Bulb replacement, electrical check
9. **Drainage Cleaning** — Clearing of catch basins, channel drains

#### Materials:
- **Cleaners:** pH-neutral detergents, sodium hypochlorite (diluted)
- **Sealants:** Acrylic, polyurethane, penetrating sealers
- **Jointing Materials:** Polymeric sand, cement sand (1:3)
- **Mortar:** Cement + sand + lime for repointing

---

### D. Water Feature Maintenance

#### Methods:
1. **Algae Control** — UV clarifiers, algaecides, barley straw
2. **Pump Servicing** — Cleaning impellers, checking seals
3. **Filter Cleaning** — Biological and mechanical filters
4. **Water Quality Testing** — pH, hardness, ammonia, nitrates
5. **Plant Pruning** — Aquatic plant management
6. **Debris Removal** — Skimming, netting

#### Materials:
- **Algaecides:** Copper-based, hydrogen peroxide
- **Test Kits:** API Freshwater Master Test Kit
- **Filter Media:** Activated carbon, ceramic rings, foam

---

### E. Irrigation System Maintenance

#### Methods:
1. **Sprinkler Head Cleaning** — Nozzle unclogging
2. **Valve Inspection** — Solenoid, check valves
3. **Pipe Leak Detection** — Pressure testing
4. **Controller Programming** — Seasonal adjustments
5. **Backflow Preventer Testing** — Annual certification
6. **Dripline Flushing** — Sediment removal
7. **Emitter Cleaning** — Acid injection (for mineral buildup)

#### Materials:
- **Replacement Parts:** Nozzles, diaphragms, risers, solenoid coils
- **Sealants:** Teflon tape, PVC primer + cement
- **Acidic Cleaners:** Phosphoric acid (for drip emitters)

---

## 2.2 Maintenance Schedule Framework

A standard institutional landscape maintenance schedule follows the **IPM Calendar**:

| Season | Primary Operations |
|--------|-------------------|
| **Spring (Mar–May)** | Aeration, dethatching, pre-emergent herbicide, spring fertilization, pruning of winter-damaged wood |
| **Summer (Jun–Aug)** | Frequent mowing, deep irrigation, pest monitoring, deadheading, mulching |
| **Autumn (Sep–Nov)** | Overseeding, core aeration, winterizer fertilization, leaf removal, planting bulbs |
| **Winter (Dec–Feb)** | Tool maintenance, planning, dormant pruning of deciduous trees, snow/ice management |

---

## 2.3 Maintenance Bids and Services — Detailed Framework

### A. Types of Maintenance Contracts

| Contract Type | Description | Risk Bearer | Typical Use |
|--------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Lump Sum (Fixed Price)** | Contractor quotes a fixed monthly/annual fee for defined scope | Contractor | Residential, small commercial |
| **Cost-Plus** | Client reimburses actual costs + markup % | Client | Large institutional projects |
| **Unit Rate (Schedule of Rates)** | Pre-agreed rates per task; client pays for actual units performed | Shared | Government/public works |
| **Performance-Based** | Payment tied to KPI achievement (turf quality scores, plant survival %) | Contractor | Premium contracts |
| **Time & Materials** | Hourly labor + material pass-through | Client | Emergency/as-needed work |

### B. Maintenance Bid Components

A complete maintenance bid document contains **8 mandatory sections**:

1. **Cover Letter & Executive Summary**
2. **Company Profile & Credentials** (Experience, licenses, certifications)
3. **Scope of Work (SOW)** — Detailed task description
4. **Methodology & Work Plan** — How tasks will be executed
5. **Schedule & Frequency Matrix** — Calendar of operations
6. **Manpower & Equipment Deployment Plan**
7. **Material Specifications** — Brands, quantities, specifications
8. **Commercial Bid** — Cost breakdown (BOQ — Bill of Quantities)

### C. Cost Estimation Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala Landscape Maintenance Cost (2024 indicative range):** **₹8–₹25 per m² per month** for institutional grade maintenance with full-service scope.

#### KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| Formula/Parameter | Expression | Application |
|-------------------|------------|-------------|
| **Total Maintenance Cost (TMC)** | $TMC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (L_i \times H_i) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} M_j + E + P + O$ | Annual bid estimation |
| **Cost per m² per month** | $C_{m^2} = \dfrac{TMC}{A \times 12}$ | Comparative benchmarking |
| **Labor Cost (Li × Hi)** | $L_i$ = labor rate (₹/hr); $H_i$ = hours/month for task i | Direct labor |
| **Material Cost (Mj)** | $\sum M_j$ = sum of all consumables | Fertilizers, mulch, chemicals |
| **Equipment Cost (E)** | $E = (D \times U) + M_{equip}$ | Depreciation + utilization + maintenance |
| **Profit Margin (P)** | $P = (TMC - Direct\;Costs) \times \dfrac{Margin\%}{100}$ | Contractor profit |
| **Overhead (O)** | $O = TMC \times \dfrac{OH\%}{100}$ | Admin, office, insurance |
| **Turf Water Requirement** | $W_t = ET_0 \times K_c \times A$ | Weekly irrigation volume |
| **Pruning Volume** | $V_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times L \times N$ | Total biomass removed (D = avg diameter, L = length, N = number) |
| **Mulch Volume** | $V_m = A \times d$ | Volume of mulch needed (A = bed area, d = depth) |

> Where:
> - $ET_0$ = Reference evapotranspiration (mm/day) — for Kerala typically **4–6 mm/day** in summer
> - $K_c$ = Crop coefficient (turf: **0.8**, shrubs: **0.5**, trees: **0.6**)
> - $A$ = Area in m²

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Turf Irrigation Water Requirement

### Problem Context
Calculate weekly irrigation water requirement for a **2,500 m² turf area** in Kochi during March, given:
- Reference evapotranspiration ($ET_0$) = 5.5 mm/day
- Crop coefficient ($K_c$) for warm-season turf = 0.8
- Effective rainfall during the week = 12 mm

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the Crop Evapotranspiration Equation**

The actual water consumed by turfgrass equals the reference evapotranspiration multiplied by the crop coefficient:

$$ET_c = ET_0 \times K_c$$

**Step 2: Substitute Known Values**

$$ET_c = 5.5 \times 0.8 = 4.4 \text{ mm/day}$$

**Step 3: Convert Daily ETc to Weekly ETc**

$$ET_{c,weekly} = ET_c \times 7 = 4.4 \times 7 = 30.8 \text{ mm/week}$$

**Step 4: Convert mm to Volume (Liters per m²)**

Since 1 mm of water over 1 m² equals 1 liter:

$$V_{required} = 30.8 \text{ L/m}^2 \text{ per week}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Total Volume for Entire Area**

$$V_{total} = V_{required} \times A = 30.8 \times 2500 = 77{,}000 \text{ L/week}$$

**Step 6: Subtract Effective Rainfall Contribution**

$$V_{irrigation} = V_{total} - (Rainfall \times A)$$

$$V_{irrigation} = 77{,}000 - (12 \times 2500) = 77{,}000 - 30{,}000 = 47{,}000 \text{ L/week}$$

**Step 7: Adjust for Irrigation Efficiency (assume 75%)**

$$V_{applied} = \dfrac{V_{irrigation}}{Efficiency} = \dfrac{47{,}000}{0.75} = 62{,}667 \text{ L/week}$$

$$\boxed{V_{applied} \approx 62.67 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per week}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mulch Volume Calculation

### Problem Context
A landscape bed of **180 m²** requires organic mulch application at a depth of **75 mm**. Calculate:
- (a) Total mulch volume required
- (b) Cost if mulch costs ₹850 per m³

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: State the Volume Formula**

$$V_m = A \times d$$

where $A$ = bed area (m²), $d$ = depth (m)

**Step 2: Convert Depth to Meters**

$$d = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into Equation**

$$V_m = 180 \times 0.075$$

$$V_m = 13.5 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Calculate Material Cost**

$$\text{Cost} = V_m \times \text{Rate per m}^3 = 13.5 \times 850$$

$$\boxed{\text{Cost} = ₹11{,}475}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maintenance Bid Cost Estimation

### Problem Context
A developer invites bids for maintenance of a **5,000 m² residential complex landscape** for **12 months**. Estimate the annual maintenance cost using the following data:

| Task | Labor (hr/visit) | Visits/month | Labor Rate (₹/hr) |
|------|------------------|--------------|-------------------|
| Mowing | 24 | 8 | 200 |
| Edging | 8 | 8 | 200 |
| Hedge trimming | 12 | 2 | 220 |
| Fertilization | 6 | 1 | 250 |
| Irrigation management | 10 | 4 | 250 |
| Pruning (trees) | 16 | 1 | 300 |

- Annual material cost: ₹85,000
- Equipment depreciation: ₹40,000/year
- Overhead: 12% of direct costs
- Contractor margin: 15% on (Direct Costs + Overhead)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Calculate Annual Labor Hours per Task**

$$\text{Annual Hours}_i = \text{hrs/visit} \times \text{visits/month} \times 12$$

**Step 2: Tabulate and Sum**

| Task | Calculation | Annual Hours | Labor Cost (₹) |
|------|------------|--------------|---------------|
| Mowing | $24 \times 8 \times 12$ | 2,304 | 460,800 |
| Edging | $8 \times 8 \times 12$ | 768 | 153,600 |
| Hedge trimming | $12 \times 2 \times 12$ | 288 | 63,360 |
| Fertilization | $6 \times 1 \times 12$ | 72 | 18,000 |
| Irrigation | $10 \times 4 \times 12$ | 480 | 120,000 |
| Pruning | $16 \times 1 \times 12$ | 192 | 57,600 |
| **Total** | | | **₹873,360** |

**Step 3: Calculate Direct Costs**

$$\text{Direct Costs} = \text{Labor} + \text{Material} + \text{Equipment}$$

$$\text{Direct Costs} = 873{,}360 + 85{,}000 + 40{,}000 = ₹998{,}360$$

**Step 4: Calculate Overhead**

$$\text{Overhead} = 998{,}360 \times 0.12 = ₹119{,}803.2$$

**Step 5: Subtotal Before Margin**

$$\text{Subtotal} = 998{,}360 + 119{,}803.2 = ₹1{,}118{,}163.2$$

**Step 6: Add Contractor Margin**

$$\text{Margin} = 1{,}118{,}163.2 \times 0.15 = ₹167{,}724.48$$

**Step 7: Final Annual Bid**

$$\boxed{\text{Annual Bid} = 1{,}118{,}163.2 + 167{,}724.48 = ₹1{,}285{,}887.68 \approx ₹12.86 \text{ Lakhs}}$$

**Step 8: Cost per m² per Month**

$$C_{m^2} = \dfrac{1{,}285{,}887.68}{5000 \times 12} \approx ₹21.43 \text{ per m}^2 \text{ per month}$$

This falls within the indicative institutional range (₹8–₹25/m²/month), validating the calculation.

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q: List any six essential operations involved in the maintenance of turfgrass in a designed landscape.**

**Model Answer:**
The six essential turfgrass maintenance operations are:
1. **Mowing** — Regular cutting following the 1/3 rule at species-specific heights (e.g., Bermuda at 20 mm)
2. **Edging** — Trimming boundaries along walkways and beds for a clean appearance
3. **Fertilization** — Scheduled application of NPK based on soil test (typically 3:1:2 ratio for turf)
4. **Irrigation** — Supplemental watering to meet ET deficit (approximately 25–30 mm/week)
5. **Aeration** — Mechanical core removal (once or twice annually) to relieve compaction
6. **Weed and Pest Control** — IPM-based herbicide and pesticide application

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often omit **dethatching** or **overseeding**, which are equally critical. Avoid limiting to "mowing and watering only."

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q: Differentiate between heading cut and thinning cut in pruning. State one use of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Heading Cut | Thinning Cut |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| Definition | Cut made through a stem at a point above a bud, removing apical dominance | Complete removal of a branch at its point of origin |
| Effect on growth | Encourages dense, bushy regrowth from lateral buds | Maintains natural form; opens canopy |
| Use | Hedges, topiary, formal shrub maintenance | Shade trees, ornamental tree care |

**Use of Heading Cut:** Maintaining formal hedge shape in *Ficus benjamina* boundary planting.
**Use of Thinning Cut:** Removing crossing/rubbing branches in mature *Delonix regia* (Gulmohar).

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Q: A 1.2-hectare institutional campus landscape in Thrissur requires annual maintenance. Prepare a detailed maintenance plan covering:**
**(a) Turf maintenance schedule and materials for a warm-season grass species** (7 Marks)
**(b) Tree and shrub pruning protocol with seasonal calendar** (7 Marks)

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Turf Maintenance Schedule and Materials

**Step 1: Site Assessment & Grass Selection**

Thrissur has a tropical climate with warm-season grass dominance. Recommend **Bermuda grass (Cynodon dactylon)** or **Bahiagrass** for the campus.

**Step 2: Annual Turf Maintenance Calendar**

| Month | Operation | Material/Specification |
|-------|-----------|------------------------|
| January | Light mowing, debris removal | Rotary mower, leaf rake |
| February | Pre-spring fertilizer (slow release) | NPK 18-18-18 @ 10 g/m² |
| March | Core aeration + topdressing | Hollow tine aerator, sand-compost 3:1 mix @ 0.5 m³/100 m² |
| April | Overseeding thin patches | Bermuda seed @ 10 g/m² |
| May | Increase mowing frequency to 2×/week | Maintain 20–25 mm height |
| June–Aug | Weekly mowing, deep irrigation 2×/week | 25 mm per session |
| September | Disease monitoring, balanced fertilizer | NPK 17-17-17 @ 15 g/m² |
| October | Dethatching if thatch > 12 mm | Vertical mower |
| November | Winterizer fertilizer (high K) | NPK 3-1-3 ratio |
| December | Reduce mowing to 1×/week, tool maintenance | — |

**Step 3: Materials Specification**
- Fertilizers: Urea, DAP, MOP, complex NPKs (ISI marked)
- Topdressing: Sharp river sand + vermicompost (3:1)
- Herbicides: 2,4-D (broadleaf), Atrazine (pre-emergent)
- Water: pH 6.5–7.5, free from industrial effluents

**Step 4: Equipment**
- Reel mower for fine turf areas
- Rotary mower for general areas
- Hose-reel sprinkler or pop-up irrigation system

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Pruning Protocol with Seasonal Calendar

**Step 1: Pruning Principles**

Follow **ANSI A300 Pruning Standards**:
- Cut just outside the branch collar
- Avoid leaving stubs
- Disinfect tools between trees (10% bleach)
- Never remove more than 25% of canopy in a single season

**Step 2: Seasonal Pruning Calendar**

| Season | Tree/Shrub Operations |
|--------|----------------------|
| **Spring (Mar–May)** | Prune winter-damaged wood; light shaping of flowering shrubs **after** bloom |
| **Summer (Jun–Aug)** | Deadheading, water-shoot removal, minimal major cuts |
| **Autumn (Sep–Nov)** | Main structural pruning window — crown thinning, deadwood removal, crown raising |
| **Winter (Dec–Feb)** | Dormant pruning for deciduous trees; formative pruning of young trees |

**Step 3: Tree-Specific Pruning Guidelines**

- *Delonix regia*: Prune after flowering (autumn); remove only crossing branches
- *Ficus benjamina*: Heading cuts acceptable for hedges; thinning for specimen trees
- *Cassia fistula*: Prune after flowering; avoid heavy cuts during monsoon
- *Bougainvillea*: Light pruning monthly; heading cuts for bushiness

**Step 4: Safety and Sanitation**
- Use PPE (gloves, helmet, eye protection)
- Cherry picker for trees > 4 m height
- Avoid pruning during active disease infection
- Apply wound paint only for oak wilt susceptible species (not generally recommended)

---

### QUESTION B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**Q: With reference to landscape maintenance contracts:**
**(a) Describe the components of a maintenance bid document in detail** (7 Marks)
**(b) Estimate the annual maintenance cost for a 3,000 m² residential complex using a cost-build-up method, assuming suitable data** (7 Marks)

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Components of a Maintenance Bid Document

A maintenance bid document contains **eight mandatory sections**:

**1. Cover Letter & Executive Summary**
Brief introduction of the firm, key highlights, and value proposition.

**2. Company Profile and Credentials**
- Years of experience
- Portfolio of similar projects
- Licenses, registrations, GST, PAN
- Team qualifications and certifications

**3. Scope of Work (SOW)**
Detailed description of tasks including:
- Area-wise breakdown
- Plant species to be maintained
- Hardscape elements (pavers, lighting)
- Water features
- Irrigation system

**4. Methodology and Work Plan**
- Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for each task
- IPM protocols
- Safety procedures
- Emergency response plan

**5. Schedule and Frequency Matrix**
- Calendar of operations per zone
- Frequency table (weekly/monthly/quarterly)
- Seasonal operations calendar

**6. Manpower and Equipment Deployment Plan**
- Number and qualification of staff
- Equipment list with make/model
- Deployment schedule

**7. Material Specifications**
- Brand names and ISI specifications
- Quantities per month/quarter
- Material safety data sheets (MSDS)

**8. Commercial Bid (Bill of Quantities — BOQ)**
- Unit rates per task
- Monthly/annual totals
- GST component
- Payment terms and escalation clauses

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Annual Maintenance Cost Estimation

**Given:**
- Area: $A = 3000 \text{ m}^2$
- Contract period: 12 months
- Service scope: Full maintenance (turf, plants, irrigation, hardscape)

**Assumed Data:**

| Component | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Annual labor hours | 2,800 hrs |
| Labor rate | ₹220/hr |
| Annual material cost | ₹60,000 |
| Equipment cost (annualized) | ₹35,000 |
| Subcontractor cost | ₹25,000 |
| Overhead % | 12% |
| Profit margin % | 15% |

**Step 1: Calculate Labor Cost**

$$\text{Labor Cost} = 2800 \times 220 = ₹616{,}000$$

**Step 2: Sum Direct Costs**

$$\text{Direct Costs} = 616{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 35{,}000 + 25{,}000 = ₹736{,}000$$

**Step 3: Apply Overhead**

$$\text{Overhead} = 736{,}000 \times 0.12 = ₹88{,}320$$

**Step 4: Subtotal**

$$\text{Subtotal} = 736{,}000 + 88{,}320 = ₹824{,}320$$

**Step 5: Apply Profit Margin (on Subtotal)**

$$\text{Margin} = 824{,}320 \times 0.15 = ₹123{,}648$$

**Step 6: Final Annual Bid**

$$\boxed{\text{Annual Bid} = 824{,}320 + 123{,}648 = ₹947{,}968 \approx ₹9.48 \text{ Lakhs}}$$

**Step 7: Cost per m² per Month**

$$C_{m^2} = \dfrac{947{,}968}{3000 \times 12} \approx ₹26.33 \text{ per m}^2 \text{ per month}$$

This is slightly above the institutional range due to the residential scope requiring higher attention frequency.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Maintenance Bid Questions:**
> 1. **Omitting components from bid document** — Students frequently forget "Schedule Matrix" or "Material Specifications," losing 1–2 marks
> 2. **Not applying overhead correctly** — Overhead is calculated on **direct costs**, not on total cost
> 3. **Margin calculation errors** — Margin is on (Direct + Overhead), not just on direct costs
> 4. **Missing unit conversions** — When using ET-based irrigation formulas, ensure mm-to-liters conversion is explicit
> 5. **Confusing heading vs thinning cuts** — A 1-mark differentiation error is common
> 6. **Forgetting GST component** — Commercial bids must mention applicable taxes separately
